unit.name: Case Packer
unit.cfj: Group and load cartoned units into shipping cases for tertiary packaging

meta.method: Ulwick ODI (Importance × Satisfaction → Opportunity), needs scoring v2

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of cartoned units from different products or lots being grouped for one shipping case
rows.plain: Avoid putting boxed units from different products or production groups into the same shipping case. This helps prevent products from being mixed up during delivery.
rows.imp_rat: Grouping different products/lots into one case is a core mixup error (relevance 9) with catastrophic recall/safety impact (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Line clearance procedures reduce commingling, but reliance on manual segregation leaves a persistent risk of mixed lots. Line clearance and segregation checks between products add setup time. Preventing mixed products depends on disciplined changeover procedures and operator vigilance. A mixed-product case is a serious, costly quality failure requiring investigation and potential recall.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting a packed case containing cartoned units from different products or lots
rows.plain: Avoid approving a shipping case that contains boxed units from different products or production groups. This helps ensure each case contains the correct items.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting a case with mixed products/lots is a critical verification failure at the acceptance gate (relevance 9) with catastrophic recall/safety consequences (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Procedural line clearance helps, but manual acceptance remains a recurring vulnerability to mixed contents. Content verification before acceptance takes additional time. Catching mixed lots at acceptance depends on operator judgment absent automated checks. Accepting a mixed-lot case is a severe quality and compliance cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of grouped cartoned units being loaded into a shipping case assigned to another product or lot
rows.plain: Make sure grouped cartons are put into the shipping case marked for the correct product and batch. This prevents products from being sent with the wrong identification.
rows.imp_rat: Loading groups into a case assigned to another product/lot is a direct mixup/mislabel error (relevance 9) with severe recall and traceability consequences (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Staging discipline and labels mitigate this, but manual matching leaves a recurring misassignment risk. Confirming case-to-product assignment adds verification time at changeovers. Correct assignment relies on operator attention to labeling and staging discipline. Loading into the wrong case triggers costly rework or product holds.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of cartoned units from different products, strengths, or lots being grouped in one shipping case
rows.plain: Prevent cartons containing different products, product strengths, or production groups from being packed together in one shipping box. This helps ensure customers receive exactly what was intended.
rows.imp_rat: Grouping different products/strengths/lots into one case is a critical mixup at the grouping step (relevance 9) with catastrophic recall/safety consequences (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Barcode verification and one-lot-per-line practices catch most, but manual grouping steps leave residual error. Grouping controls are performed inline without meaningful added delay. Correct grouping still leans on vigilant operators and clear setup instructions. Wrong grouping wastes materials and labor when detected downstream.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a shipping case contains cartoned units from different products, strengths, or lots
rows.plain: Prevent a shipping box from containing cartons with different products, product strengths, or production groups. This helps avoid sending customers the wrong combination of products.
rows.imp_rat: Mixing products/strengths/lots in one case is a classic product-mixup, central to correct grouping (relevance 9); severity is catastrophic (10) with recall and patient-safety exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Physical segregation, line clearance and vision/scanning reduce but do not eliminate commingling risk. Line clearance and changeover checks take time but are embedded in normal cadence. Preventing mixed-product cases depends heavily on operator attention and segregation discipline. A mixed case triggers costly rework or rejection, motivating existing controls.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a packed lot enters shipment flow before final disposition approval
rows.plain: Do not let a packed batch be shipped before the final approval is given. This prevents unapproved product from reaching customers.
rows.imp_rat: A lot entering shipment before disposition approval bypasses the release gate (relevance 9) and can send unapproved product to market with catastrophic compliance consequences (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Quarantine locations and ERP status locks make premature shipment-flow entry infrequent. Disposition hold delays shipment slightly but is standard workflow. Enforcement relies on QA and warehouse discipline more than specialized skill. Premature shipment can force costly retrieval, so systemic holds are maintained.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a packed lot failing release criteria is approved for release
rows.plain: Prevent a packed batch that does not meet the requirements for release from being approved for shipment or use.
rows.imp_rat: Releasing a failing lot defeats the quality-gate purpose central to the packing job (relevance 9); severity is maximal (10) because non-conforming product reaches market, risking recall and patient harm.
rows.sat_rat: Mature QMS release-gate controls and electronic status locks make approving a failing lot uncommon though not impossible. Disposition gating adds review time but is a routine step, not a chronic delay. Release decisions rely on trained QA judgement that most sites reliably staff. An erroneous release is costly through recall, but formal hold-and-release controls limit occurrence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of materials from different batches or component lots being commingled at the case-packing station
rows.plain: Keep materials from different production batches or part groups from being mixed at the box-packing station. Mixing them can cause products to be packed with the wrong materials or records.
rows.imp_rat: Commingling different batches/lots at the station is a direct mixup at the source (relevance 8) with catastrophic recall/traceability consequences (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Line clearance procedures reduce commingling, but station-level mixing remains a persistent manual-control risk. Segregating batches and lots adds line-clearance time. Preventing commingling depends on disciplined material handling and vigilance. Commingled lots create costly quality investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartoned units incorrectly grouped or loaded after restart
rows.plain: Reduce the share of boxed units that are put into the wrong groups or loaded incorrectly after the line starts again. This helps prevent packaging mix-ups after a stoppage.
rows.imp_rat: Units incorrectly grouped/loaded after restart is a core correctness error at a vulnerable transition (relevance 8) with serious escape potential (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Restart is a known weak point where mis-grouping spikes, and status-quo purge-and-check routines are inconsistently applied. Post-restart errors surface quickly during the first cases after resumption but only after some defective cases are produced. Clean restarts depend on operator discipline to purge and verify, an error-prone manual judgement. The first cases after restart are frequently scrapped, a small but repeated material and labor loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of rejected or quarantined cases transferred into releasable inventory
rows.plain: Keep rejected or held cases from being moved into the stock that is approved for use or shipment. This helps prevent unsuitable product from reaching customers.
rows.imp_rat: Rejected/quarantined cases transferred into releasable inventory is a critical containment breach (relevance 8); defective product entering saleable stock is catastrophic (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Physical quarantine and ERP status locks reliably prevent most reject transfers into inventory. Segregation handling of rejects adds minor time. Keeping rejects out relies on procedural discipline more than skill. Reject-to-releasable transfer risks recall and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting a packed case whose external identity does not match its contents
rows.plain: Make sure the outside label of a packed case matches what is actually inside. This helps prevent the wrong product from being shipped or used.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting a case whose external identity mismatches contents is a core acceptance error (relevance 8) leading to serious mislabeling and recall exposure (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Barcode verification systems make this highly reliable where deployed, but manual label matching still allows recurring mismatches. Label-to-content checks add brief time at the closing point. Matching external identity to contents requires operator care unless scanned automatically. Identity mismatches cause costly downstream recalls and holds.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting a packed case with an incorrect carton quantity
rows.plain: Avoid accepting a packed case that contains the wrong number of cartons. This helps ensure customers receive the quantity they were supposed to get.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting a case with incorrect carton quantity is a verification failure central to the job (relevance 9); it causes downstream count discrepancies and returns (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Count sensors and checkweighers catch most errors where installed, but manual acceptance decisions remain a recurring weak point. Verification checks add a small time cost at the acceptance point. Detecting an incorrect quantity before acceptance leans on operator diligence unless automated counting is installed. Accepting a wrong-count case propagates costly downstream corrections and complaints.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of foreign material or visible contamination inside a packed case remaining undetected
rows.plain: Make sure no foreign objects or visible dirt inside a packed case go unnoticed. This helps prevent contaminated or damaged products from being accepted.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected foreign material/contamination inside a packed case is a critical contamination escape (relevance 7) with severe patient-safety/recall consequences (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Manual visual inspection is the primary control and routinely misses contamination, leaving a persistent gap. Inspecting for contamination inside packed cases adds time before closure. Detecting foreign material or contamination reliably requires keen, sustained operator attention that is hard to maintain. Undetected contamination causes costly recalls.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of shipping cases released with unresolved grouping, loading, closure, or protection deviations
rows.plain: Do not release shipping boxes when there are still unresolved problems with how items are grouped, loaded, closed, or protected. This helps products arrive safely and in the expected condition.
rows.imp_rat: Releasing cases with unresolved grouping/loading/closure/protection deviations bypasses quality gates (relevance 8) and can send non-conforming shipments to market (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Deviation-closure gates exist but real gaps persist where minor deviations are informally cleared. Holding cases with open deviations delays release but is procedurally required. Judging whether a deviation is resolved requires quality expertise that can be inconsistently applied. Releasing with unresolved deviations risks costly downstream failure or recall.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of carton groups containing more or fewer units than the specified case quantity
rows.plain: Have fewer cartons or groups with the wrong number of units in them. This helps ensure each carton contains the amount that was requested.
rows.imp_rat: Getting the specified case quantity right is the essence of grouping (relevance 9); wrong counts cause shipment errors and reconciliation failures (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Fixed pockets, indexing conveyors and count sensors make correct quantity fairly consistent, though manual grouping still produces occasional miscounts. Counting to the specified case quantity is quick when guides or count sensors are present but slows manual stations. Accurate counting relies on operator vigilance, which degrades over long shifts. Wrong-count cases waste materials and labor when reopened and corrected.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of released shipping cases with incorrect, unreadable, or mismatched identification
rows.plain: Prevent shipped cases from having wrong, unreadable, or mismatched labels or tracking information. Correct identification helps ensure the right products reach the right destination.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect/unreadable/mismatched case identification breaks traceability core to the job (relevance 8); mislabeled shipments carry severe recall/safety exposure (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Print-and-apply with verification catches most defects, though print degradation and manual labels leave gaps. Identification verification is a quick inline scan. Correct, readable ID depends on setup and print-quality monitoring. Mismatched or unreadable ID causes rework and scanning failures downstream.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shipping cases whose identification does not match the enclosed product or lot
rows.plain: Make sure the shipping label matches the product and batch inside the package. This helps prevent the wrong product from being sent or used.
rows.imp_rat: Case identification mismatching contents is central to traceability of the packing job (relevance 8); mislabeled shipments create serious recall and safety exposure (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Automated label print-and-apply with scan verification substantially reduces mismatches though manual overrides remain a gap. Label verification is a quick inline step. Correct case ID depends on operator setup and reconciliation attention. Mismatched identification causes rework and potential rejection costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of prepared shipping cases that do not match the specified product and carton grouping
rows.plain: Reduce the number of shipping cases that contain the wrong product or the wrong arrangement of cartons. This helps ensure shipments are packed as ordered.
rows.imp_rat: Prepared cases not matching specified product/grouping is a mixup precursor central to correctness (relevance 8) with serious escape potential (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Staging and changeover checks reduce mismatches, but manual preparation leaves recurring errors. Reworking mismatched prepared cases interrupts flow. Preparing the right case for the right grouping relies on staging discipline and attention. Mismatched prepared cases waste materials and labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shipping cases packed outside the approved grouping and loading configuration after restart
rows.plain: After a restart, make sure shipping cases are packed and loaded in the approved way. This helps prevent incorrect packing or unstable loads.
rows.imp_rat: Packing outside approved configuration after restart is a core correctness error (relevance 8); restart states are a common escape point with serious consequences (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: First-article checks after restart catch most nonconforming cases but drift and misjudgement still let some through. Confirming the grouping and loading configuration after restart takes a short inspection window. Judging conformance to the approved configuration depends on trained visual assessment. Cases packed out of configuration must be broken down, wasting labor and material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that affected cartoned units become mixed with acceptable units during stoppage resolution
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that damaged or questionable boxed items get mixed back in with acceptable ones when a production line is restarted. This helps prevent unsuitable products from being treated as good.
rows.imp_rat: Affected units mixing with acceptable ones during stoppage resolution is a mixup during recovery (relevance 7); allows defective product into good stock, high severity (8).
rows.sat_rat: Segregation practice exists but under stoppage pressure suspect and good units do get commingled, so containment is only moderately reliable. Segregating affected units adds handling time during resolution. Proper quarantine relies on operators knowing which units are suspect, an attention-dependent judgement. Mixing forces broader rejection or investigation, escalating waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of overlooking mixed packaging material identities or lots in the station supply
rows.plain: Make sure different packaging materials or batches are not mixed together at the work station. This helps prevent the wrong materials from being used.
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking mixed material identities/lots in station supply is a mixup-detection failure (relevance 7) with serious recall/safety severity (8).
rows.sat_rat: Visual checks are the main control, leaving recurring risk that mixed identities go unnoticed. Checking supply for mixed identities adds brief inspection time. Spotting mixed material identities in the supply demands sustained operator attention and label literacy. Overlooked mixed materials cause costly rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of packaging materials added after verification bypassing confirmation against active batch requirements
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that packaging materials added later skip the check against the requirements for the current batch. This helps prevent the wrong materials from being used.
rows.imp_rat: Materials added after verification bypassing confirmation is a mixup gate bypass (relevance 7); unverified materials risk serious mislabel/recall (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Procedures exist but post-verification additions frequently bypass confirmation, a recurring control gap. Reverifying materials added after initial checks costs extra time. Remembering to confirm post-verification additions depends on operator discipline. Unverified additions risk costly wrong-material use.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the case packer is configured for a format differing from the packaging order
rows.plain: The box-packing machine should be set up for the same package size and style listed in the order. A mismatch could lead to incorrect packing.
rows.imp_rat: A machine configured for the wrong format directly derails the packing job (relevance 8) and can produce entire runs of mispacked cases before detection (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-based changeover with order paperwork catches most mismatches but manual selection still lets occasional wrong-format setups through. Verifying the loaded format against the order takes only moments today via HMI recipe selection but is often skipped under time pressure. Correct format selection depends heavily on operator attention and familiarity with recipe naming conventions. A wrong format run wastes cases and cartons and forces rework, a cost operators actively try to avoid.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of staging an incorrect case, divider, or label variant for the active batch
rows.plain: Workers need to choose the correct box, divider, and label for the batch being prepared. Using the wrong items could cause the products to be packed or identified incorrectly.
rows.imp_rat: Staging an incorrect case/divider/label variant for the batch is a mixup/mislabel precursor (relevance 7) with serious recall consequences (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Staging checklists mitigate errors, but manual staging still allows recurring wrong-variant events. Confirming the correct variant at staging adds setup time. Staging the right variant relies on operator interpretation of batch records. Wrong-variant staging wastes materials and forces rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of potentially affected cases omitted from containment
rows.plain: Make sure all products or cases that might be affected by a quality problem are contained. This prevents potentially affected items from being used or distributed.
rows.imp_rat: Cases omitted from containment defeats containment of suspect product (relevance 7); allows potentially defective product to escape, high severity (8).
rows.sat_rat: Containment procedures exist but scoping completeness is inconsistent and a recurring escape source. Containment scoping can delay while affected units are identified. Defining containment scope demands experienced investigator judgement. Omitted cases risk expensive escapes and recalls.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that documented case-packing or shipping-integrity defects are omitted from disposition assessment
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that known packing or shipping defects are left out when deciding whether a production lot can be released. Every relevant defect should be considered before the decision is made.
rows.imp_rat: Documented defects omitted from disposition assessment is a review-completeness failure (relevance 7); overlooked defects can lead to release of non-conforming product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Deviation logs feed disposition, but manual handoffs let some documented defects slip from assessment. Assembling defects for disposition adds review time. Ensuring all defects reach assessment relies on reviewer thoroughness. Omitted defects risk downstream failure and recall costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of released shipping cases with an incorrect carton count or loading configuration
rows.plain: Prevent shipped cases from having the wrong number of cartons or the wrong arrangement inside. This reduces the chance that customers receive incomplete or incorrectly packed shipments.
rows.imp_rat: Releasing cases with incorrect carton count/loading is a core escape from the packing job (relevance 8) causing shipment discrepancies and returns (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Automated counting and check-weighing make incorrect counts fairly uncommon at release. Count verification is fast, often automated inline. Correct count relies on setup and sensor calibration more than skill. Wrong counts cause reconciliation waste and customer credits.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of applying an incorrect case quantity to the active product and case configuration
rows.plain: Reduce the chance of using the wrong number of items for the product and box arrangement being packed. Correct counts help ensure each box contains what it should.
rows.imp_rat: Applying an incorrect case quantity to the active configuration corrupts core grouping accuracy (relevance 8); causes systematic count errors across a run (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-driven machine settings make this consistent on automated lines, but manual configuration remains a recurring error source. Confirming the correct case quantity setting at changeover adds setup time. Applying the right quantity depends on correctly reading the batch record and configuring the station. An incorrect quantity applied across a run wastes materials and forces rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that potentially affected cases are released before deviation impact is established
rows.plain: Hold back any cases that might be affected until the impact of the problem is understood. This prevents possibly unsuitable cases from being released.
rows.imp_rat: Releasing potentially affected cases before deviation impact is established defeats containment (relevance 7); can lead to distribution of suspect product and recalls (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Containment practices exist but timely and complete impact assessment before release is inconsistently achieved. Containment decisions can delay release while impact is assessed. Establishing deviation impact demands investigator judgement that is a scarce resource. Releasing affected cases prematurely risks expensive recall exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of cartoned units linked to the wrong shipping-case parent across the packaging sequence
rows.plain: Keep the number of cartons assigned to the wrong shipping case as low as possible during packaging. Correct links make it possible to trace each carton to the case it was packed in.
rows.imp_rat: Units linked to the wrong shipping-case parent corrupts serialization/traceability (relevance 7); breaks track-and-trace with serious recall/regulatory impact (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Vision-based aggregation and serialization systems are mature and widely deployed, handling correct parenting in most runs, though sensor misreads and buffer mismatches still cause occasional wrong-parent linkages. Parent-child association is generated in real time during packing so latency is rarely the issue. Configuring aggregation logic and camera/scanner triggers correctly requires serialization specialists, and misconfiguration is a common source of wrong linkages. Mislinked units can scrap whole cases or trigger recalls, making the cost exposure of this error high.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of released shipping cases containing foreign material introduced during grouping or loading
rows.plain: Reduce the number of shipped cases that contain unwanted material added while products are being grouped or loaded. This helps prevent contaminated or damaged shipments from reaching customers.
rows.imp_rat: Released cases with foreign material introduced during grouping/loading is a contamination escape (relevance 7) with high safety/recall severity (8).
rows.sat_rat: GMP housekeeping and inspection reduce but cannot fully guarantee absence of foreign material at case level. Foreign-material checks add minor inspection time. Detection relies on operator vigilance and housekeeping discipline. Foreign material causes rejection, complaints and possible recall costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that carton or container damage capable of compromising sterile-product integrity remains unassessed
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that damaged cartons or containers that could harm the sterility of the product are left without review. Finding and assessing this damage helps protect the product from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Unassessed damage that could compromise sterile integrity is peripheral to grouping mechanics (relevance 6) but a compromised sterile barrier is a critical patient-safety event (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Sterile-integrity damage checks are mature and heavily regulated, so this need is fairly well served though rare gaps remain. Assessing damage that could compromise sterile integrity is embedded in existing protocols without major time penalty. Judging which damage compromises sterile-product integrity requires specialized packaging and sterility knowledge. Missed damage assessment risks recalls whose cost dwarfs the assessment effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of foreign material remaining inside a prepared shipping case
rows.plain: Make sure no dirt or other unwanted material is left inside a prepared shipping box. This helps keep what is shipped clean and safe.
rows.imp_rat: Foreign material remaining inside a prepared case is a contamination error prior to loading (relevance 7) with high safety/recall severity (8).
rows.sat_rat: Case inversion/blow-off and inspection reduce residual foreign material, but detection still depends on imperfect manual checks. Inspecting prepared cases for foreign material adds minor time. Detecting foreign material inside cases requires operator inspection diligence. Foreign material events are costly quality failures.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shipping cases containing an incorrect carton count after changeover
rows.plain: Fewer shipping cases should contain the wrong number of boxed products after switching the line to a different product size or format. Accurate counts help ensure customers receive the expected quantity.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect carton count after changeover is a core count-defect at a known escape point (relevance 8); causes shipment discrepancies (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Where count sensors or checkweighers exist detection is strong, but many stations rely on manual sampling that misses intermittent count errors. Counting cartons per case is fast but continuous verification is not practical, so checks are periodic. Detecting a short or over count requires attentive counting that fatigues operators. Incorrect carton counts trigger reworked cases and potential customer chargebacks.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of foreign material being introduced into shipping cases during grouping or loading
rows.plain: Keep dirt or other unwanted material from getting into shipping boxes while they are being grouped or loaded. This helps ensure the boxes and their contents stay clean and meet required rules.
rows.imp_rat: Foreign material introduced during grouping/loading is a contamination error affecting integrity (relevance 7) with serious safety/recall consequences (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Area control and line clearance mitigate introduction, yet ambient and handling sources leave residual risk. Preventive housekeeping is time-bounded within normal operations. Prevention depends on disciplined line clearance and attention. Contamination introduces material and investigation waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartoned units or sterile barriers damaged by case-loading and distribution forces
rows.plain: Fewer boxed products or protective sterile coverings should be damaged while cases are loaded or shipped. Preventing this damage helps products arrive usable and protected.
rows.imp_rat: Damage to units or sterile barriers from loading/distribution forces undermines product integrity (relevance 7); compromised sterile barriers carry high patient-safety severity (8).
rows.sat_rat: Sterile-barrier validation and dye/burst testing are well established, but subtle case-loading damage can still evade routine checks. Verifying sterile-barrier integrity after loading forces added inspection and test time. Assessing damage to sterile barriers demands regulated device-packaging expertise and validated test methods. Damaged sterile barriers cause costly scrap and potential recalls that current controls only partly prevent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of rejected cartoned units remaining in the active tally after exclusion from packing
rows.plain: Fewer rejected boxed products should still be included in the current count after they have been removed from packing. This keeps the production total accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Rejected units remaining in the active tally corrupts count accuracy (relevance 7); leads to incorrect case quantities and reconciliation errors (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Reject handling is largely manual and reject tally errors persist as a recurring reconciliation problem. Adjusting the tally for rejected units adds reconciliation time. Correctly excluding rejects from the tally requires operator diligence. Miscounted rejects distort reconciliation and yield.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of case packing proceeding under obsolete or unapproved instructions
rows.plain: Make sure case packing does not continue using old or unapproved instructions. This helps ensure products are packed according to current, authorized directions.
rows.imp_rat: Packing under obsolete/unapproved instructions is a control failure central to correct output (relevance 8); can systematically produce non-conforming cases (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Controlled document systems and issued batch records make running on obsolete instructions uncommon. Verifying current instructions is a quick pre-run check. Version control reduces reliance on operator judgement about currency. Obsolete instructions cause costly nonconforming output.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of foreign material entering shipping cases during loading
rows.plain: Keep dirt or other unwanted material from getting into shipping boxes while they are being loaded. This helps protect the products during shipping.
rows.imp_rat: Foreign material entering cases during loading is a contamination error affecting product integrity (relevance 7) with high safety/recall severity (8).
rows.sat_rat: Good housekeeping and covered stations control most foreign material, though open loading points leave residual risk. Foreign material checks add minor inspection time during loading. Preventing foreign material entry depends on housekeeping discipline and operator awareness. Foreign material incidents are costly quality events but relatively infrequent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required case checks skipped or invalidly documented before release
rows.plain: Make sure every required check of the finished boxes is completed and recorded correctly before the products are released. Skipped or unreliable checks could allow faulty products to leave the facility.
rows.imp_rat: Skipped or invalidly documented case checks before release undermine the verification gate (relevance 7) with serious compliance/escape consequences (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: SOP-mandated checks and review catch many gaps, but skipped or backfilled entries persist under production pressure. Performing and documenting checks consumes measurable line time. Valid documentation depends on operator training and attentiveness. Skipped checks that surface later drive costly rework and review.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting packaging materials with an incorrect identity, specification, or market variant
rows.plain: Make sure the packaging materials are the correct type, design, and version before using them. This prevents products from being packed with the wrong materials or labeling.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting packaging materials with incorrect identity/spec/variant is a material-mixup gate failure (relevance 7) with serious mislabel/recall consequences (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Material issue controls and label checks make wrong-material acceptance fairly uncommon, though manual verification leaves residual risk. Verifying material identity at receipt adds modest time before use. Confirming correct specification and market variant requires attention to labels and specs. Wrong material accepted causes costly rework and scrap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of released shipping cases lacking evidence of required closure, identification, or contents checks
rows.plain: Release fewer shipping boxes unless there is proof that they were properly closed, identified, and checked for the right contents. This helps ensure shipments are complete and correctly documented.
rows.imp_rat: Releasing cases lacking evidence of required checks is a documentation-integrity failure central to release (relevance 7); creates significant compliance/recall exposure (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Batch-record requirements drive evidence capture, but omissions in manual records remain a recurring gap. Recording checks adds documentation time to the packing flow. Evidence completeness relies on operator documentation discipline. Missing evidence forces costly investigation or re-inspection.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartoned units passing the grouping point without addition to the active case tally
rows.plain: Reduce the number of boxed units that pass the grouping point without being added to the current case count. This keeps the recorded count equal to the actual number of units.
rows.imp_rat: Units passing the grouping point without being counted corrupts the case tally (relevance 7); miscounts cause shipment discrepancies and traceability gaps (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Automated counters help, but manual tally at the grouping point leaves recurring untallied units. Reconciling untallied units after the grouping point adds time. Ensuring every unit is tallied depends on operator or sensor discipline. Untallied units create reconciliation discrepancies and potential loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting packaging materials whose approved release status cannot be confirmed
rows.plain: Avoid accepting packing materials when their approved status cannot be confirmed. This helps ensure that only authorized materials are used.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting materials whose release status is unconfirmed bypasses material control (relevance 7); use of unreleased materials carries serious compliance/recall risk (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Release-status labeling and staging controls make unconfirmed status uncommon, though gaps persist with manual checks. Checking release status adds brief verification time. Confirming release status depends on operator access to status systems or labels. Using unreleased material risks costly quarantine and rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartoned units with unreadable identity, lot, or serialization data after loading or distribution
rows.plain: Keep product labels and tracking information readable and correct after products are packed or shipped. This helps products remain identifiable throughout handling and delivery.
rows.imp_rat: Unreadable identity/lot/serialization data after loading breaks traceability (relevance 7); loss of serialization has serious regulatory and recall consequences (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Inline verification and label placement standards make unreadable data uncommon but not eliminated after distribution abrasion. Post-loading readability verification adds inspection time. Ensuring codes survive loading and distribution needs coding and vision expertise. Unreadable identity/serialization data causes rejects and compliance costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shipping cases containing a carton quantity that differs from approved packing requirements
rows.plain: Make sure every shipping case contains the approved number of cartons. This prevents shipments from being packed incorrectly.
rows.imp_rat: Carton quantity differing from approved packing requirements is central to grouping correctness (relevance 8); creates count discrepancies downstream (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Count verification against master data is reliable but setup-selection errors leave a residual gap. Quantity verification is a rapid automated or manual step. Conformance to approved quantity relies on correct recipe/setup selection. Quantity deviations cause reconciliation loss and rejection.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shipping cases containing an incorrect carton count or arrangement
rows.plain: Reduce the number of shipping cases that have the wrong number of cartons or have them arranged incorrectly. This helps ensure customers receive complete and properly packed shipments.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect carton count or arrangement in cases is a core packing defect (relevance 8) producing shipment errors and rework (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Validated pack patterns and check-weighing reliably catch most arrangement errors. Arrangement checks are performed quickly at case formation. Correct arrangement depends on pack-pattern setup and operator verification. Incorrect arrangement risks damage and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of prior-batch materials remaining in the case-packing area after line clearance
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that materials from an earlier batch are left in the case-packing area after it has been cleared. This helps prevent mix-ups between batches.
rows.imp_rat: Prior-batch materials remaining after line clearance is a classic mixup/contamination risk (relevance 7) with serious recall/safety severity (8).
rows.sat_rat: Documented dual-verified line clearance is mature practice that reliably removes prior-batch materials. Line clearance consumes defined changeover time. Effective clearance relies on disciplined checklist execution. Residual materials cause mix-up rejection costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation from the required carton orientation and arrangement within each shipping case
rows.plain: Place the cartons as close as possible to the required direction and arrangement inside each shipping case. This helps keep the cases stable and correctly packed.
rows.imp_rat: Correct carton orientation/arrangement is core to properly loading cases (relevance 8); deviations cause fit/closure and protection issues but are usually caught and reworked (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Semi-automatic case packers and pattern guides handle most cases consistently, but manual stations still see recurring orientation deviations. Manual pattern arrangement adds handling time whenever cartons must be reoriented before loading. Correct orientation depends heavily on operator attention and familiarity with the case pattern, making it error-prone during fatigue or line speed increases. Misarranged cases can be reworked at little material cost but consume labor time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting a packed case with an incomplete or unsecured closure
rows.plain: Reduce the chance of accepting a shipping case that is not fully closed or is not securely fastened. This helps keep the contents protected during handling and transport.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting a case with incomplete/unsecured closure is an acceptance-gate failure (relevance 7); leads to case opening and product loss in transit (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Automatic tapers/gluers seal consistently, but incomplete closures still occur and slip past manual acceptance occasionally. Checking closure integrity adds a brief step at case discharge. Detecting incomplete closures relies on operator inspection unless sensors verify seals. Unsecured cases risk product loss and rejection downstream.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of packed shipping cases with closure pattern, position, or coverage outside specification
rows.plain: Reduce the number of shipping boxes whose closures are in the wrong pattern or position, or do not cover the required area. This helps keep packed cases secure and consistently closed.
rows.imp_rat: Closure pattern/position/coverage out of spec undermines case integrity core to the job (relevance 7); risks case opening in transit (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Sealers apply consistent closures when maintained, but drift produces recurring out-of-spec seals. Adjusting closure quality adds minor time at the sealer. Proper closure pattern depends on machine setup rather than operator skill. Out-of-spec closures waste sealing material and cause rejects.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a prepared shipping-case bottom closure failing under the specified carton load
rows.plain: Make sure the bottom flap of a prepared shipping box is unlikely to come open under the weight it must carry. This helps keep the shipment secure.
rows.imp_rat: A bottom closure failing under load causes catastrophic dropout during handling (relevance 7); product loss and damage make severity substantial (7).
rows.sat_rat: Bottom sealers and formed-case designs make bottom failure uncommon, though poorly sealed cases still fail occasionally. Verifying bottom closure integrity adds negligible time when forming cases. Proper bottom sealing depends on correct machine setup or operator forming technique. A failed bottom drops product, a costly loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a closed shipping case opening during downstream handling
rows.plain: Keep sealed shipping cases from opening while they are being moved or handled later. This protects the contents from damage or loss.
rows.imp_rat: A closed case opening during downstream handling defeats the protective job (relevance 7); leads to spillage and product damage (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Modern sealers give consistent closure, so cases opening in downstream handling is relatively uncommon. Ensuring secure closure adds little time at sealing. Reliable closure depends on machine tape/glue application rather than operator skill. Cases opening downstream cause product loss and handling costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that carton grouping count or orientation differs from the scheduled case configuration
rows.plain: Make sure the number of cartons and the way they face match the planned case arrangement. This helps the finished cases be packed correctly and consistently.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation of grouping count/orientation from the scheduled configuration affects core correctness (relevance 8); typically produces fit/rework issues rather than escapes (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Recipe presets plus first-case inspection catch many errors, but subtle orientation faults still slip past manual verification. Confirming grouping count and orientation against the case spec is a quick visual check but adds setup minutes when done rigorously. Reading the case configuration correctly requires trained interpretation of grouping patterns that less-experienced operators get wrong. Miscounted or misoriented groups generate scrapped cases and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of packed cases failing shipping-integrity acceptance criteria under routine production conditions
rows.plain: Reduce how often packed products fail checks that show whether they can stay safely sealed during shipping. This helps products arrive without damaged packaging.
rows.imp_rat: Packed cases failing shipping-integrity criteria under routine conditions undermines the protective job (relevance 7); leads to distribution damage (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Validated case designs perform consistently, but real production variability still yields periodic integrity failures. Integrity acceptance testing is periodic, not per-case delay. Interpreting acceptance criteria needs packaging engineering judgement. Failures under routine conditions drive costly rework and complaints.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shipping cases with closures that open or loosen during distribution
rows.plain: Reduce the share of shipping packages whose closures open or become loose while being transported. This helps keep the contents protected until they arrive.
rows.imp_rat: Closures that open during distribution undermine the case's protective job (relevance 7); leads to product loss and damage in transit (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Sealer qualification and closure checks limit failures, but tape/glue variation still yields distribution openings. Closure verification adds minimal time inline. Reliable closure depends on equipment setup and operator checks more than deep skill. Failed closures in distribution cause product loss and complaint costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of released shipping cases containing cartoned units damaged during grouping or loading
rows.plain: Reduce how often shipped packages contain products that were damaged while being grouped or loaded. This helps prevent damaged products from reaching customers.
rows.imp_rat: Released cases containing units damaged during grouping/loading is a product-integrity escape (relevance 7); damaged product reaches customers (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Machine guarding and gentle-handling setups reduce damage, though residual mechanical damage still occurs. Damage inspection adds modest handling time. Detecting handling damage relies on operator inspection attention. Damaged units cause rejection and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that case-packer restart is authorized before quality and safety conditions are satisfied
rows.plain: A stopped packing machine should not be restarted until the required quality and safety checks are complete. This helps prevent unsafe or defective products from moving forward.
rows.imp_rat: Authorizing restart before quality/safety conditions are met risks unsafe or non-conforming operation (relevance 6); can cause injury or defect escapes (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Restart checklists and supervisor sign-off give reasonable assurance, though under throughput pressure conditions are sometimes waved through. Restart authorization gates add some delay while checks are confirmed. Judging whether quality and safety conditions are truly met requires experienced supervisory judgement. Premature restart risks scrap and incidents, but the check itself costs little.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of loaded cases failing closure, stacking, or distribution-handling requirements
rows.plain: Reduce the share of filled packages that fail requirements for closing, stacking, or being handled during delivery. This helps packages stay secure from the workplace to their destination.
rows.imp_rat: Cases failing closure/stacking/handling requirements affect the job's protective purpose (relevance 7); consequences are distribution damage and returns (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Standardized transit-test protocols are mature and widely used, giving good but not complete assurance against handling failures. Distribution and stacking testing (ISTA/ASTM) adds weeks to configuration approval. Designing and interpreting transport-simulation tests requires specialized packaging-lab competence. Failed distribution testing means redesign and repeat testing, and field failures cost far more.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shipping cases containing a carton quantity outside the specified grouping
rows.plain: Reduce the number of shipping cases that contain the wrong number of cartons. Correct groupings help ensure orders are complete and shipments are accepted.
rows.imp_rat: Cases containing carton quantity outside spec is a core count-defect (relevance 8); wrong quantities cause shipment and inventory errors (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Count sensors, checkweighing, and vision are mature and make quantity errors rare in the specified grouping. Count verification is fast with sensors or checkweighers and adds little time. Specifying and monitoring carton quantity is routine and needs modest skill. Wrong quantities cause reship and rework costs that count-control largely prevents.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical product-protection, case-integrity, and handling requirements omitted from evaluation criteria
rows.plain: Include all important requirements for protecting the product, keeping the package intact, and handling it safely. This helps prevent damage or loss of protection.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting critical protection/integrity/handling requirements from evaluation criteria is a validation-scope gap (relevance 5); an incomplete criteria set lets serious failures escape (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Checklists and standards exist, but coverage depends on the specifier and important criteria are still routinely omitted. Building complete evaluation criteria upfront takes time but prevents later loops. Knowing every product-protection, integrity, and handling requirement to include demands broad cross-functional expertise. Omitted criteria cause missed defects that cost far more downstream than thorough criteria-setting.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of departures from validated instructions during case packing
rows.plain: Make sure workers follow the approved instructions when packing cases, with fewer departures from those instructions. This helps keep the packing process consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Departures from validated instructions during packing undermine process control (relevance 6); unvalidated operation risks compliance and defect escape (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: SOP discipline and supervision limit departures, but production pressure produces recurring deviations. Following validated instructions does not add time when adhered to. Adherence depends on training and supervision to prevent shortcuts. Departures cause deviations and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a required format component is absent or incorrectly seated when readiness is declared
rows.plain: Before declaring the setup ready, make sure every required part is present and properly fitted. This prevents missing or loose parts from causing problems later.
rows.imp_rat: A missing/misseated format component when readiness is declared risks faulty startup (relevance 6); consequences are jams or defective initial output, usually caught (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Reliance on manual visual confirmation without sensors means absent or partially seated parts are a recurring, under-detected failure. Checking that every format component is seated adds inspection time before readiness is declared. Knowing which components must be present and how they seat correctly demands experienced judgement rather than a simple checklist. A missing or misseated part discovered mid-run causes jams and wasted material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of shipping-case material losing protective strength during distribution
rows.plain: The material used for shipping boxes should be less likely to lose its protective strength while products are being transported. This helps the boxes continue protecting what is inside.
rows.imp_rat: Case material losing protective strength during distribution defeats the protective function (relevance 6); leads to collapse and product damage in transit (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Incoming material specs and supplier controls help, but humidity, lot variation and handling degrade protection unpredictably. Material qualification is upfront, not a per-run time cost. Assessing protective strength requires packaging specialist expertise. Weak material causes distribution damage and product loss costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartoned units damaged by compression or contact within the specified case arrangement
rows.plain: Prevent boxes of products from being crushed or damaged by squeezing or rubbing against other boxes in the planned case arrangement. This keeps the products in saleable condition.
rows.imp_rat: Compression/contact damage within the case arrangement affects product protection (relevance 6); damaged units mean waste and possible sterile compromise (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Compression testing and dividers reduce contact damage, but marginal arrangements still yield occasional crushed units. Detecting compression or contact damage requires added conditioning and inspection time. Judging compression tolerance within an arrangement needs knowledge of stacking strength and product fragility. Compression damage produces scrap and returns that erode margins under today's practice.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shipping cases that cannot be closed correctly with the specified carton arrangement
rows.plain: Reduce the share of shipping boxes that cannot be closed properly with the required arrangement of cartons. This helps packages fit securely and ship as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Cases that cannot close correctly halt the packing job (relevance 7); leads to rework and protection risk if forced (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Sealer settings and pattern validation resolve most closure issues, yet edge cases still slip through into runs. Closure problems typically appear during trials and force time-consuming redesign loops. Predicting whether a case will close correctly with a given arrangement takes packaging expertise and hands-on validation. Cases that won't close correctly are scrapped or reworked, wasting product and materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartoned units damaged during grouping or loading into shipping cases during the shift
rows.plain: Reduce the number of boxed products that are damaged while being grouped together or loaded into larger shipping boxes during the work shift. This helps keep products usable and avoids slowing down work.
rows.imp_rat: Units damaged during grouping/loading in the shift affects product integrity (relevance 6); results in waste and potential escape (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Mechanical handling is reasonably repeatable but jams and mis-timed loading still cause intermittent damage. Damage is typically found at inspection, adding rework time after the fact. Avoiding crush and jam damage depends on correct machine adjustment and attentive operation. Damaged cartoned units are scrapped, directly wasting finished product value.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that incomplete or conflicting carton data are used to define shipping-case requirements
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that missing or conflicting carton information is used when deciding how cases for shipping should be made. Clear information helps ensure the cases are designed correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete/conflicting carton data used to define case requirements is a specification-input error (relevance 5); bad requirements can cause systematic misfit/defects (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Data reviews exist but siloed, out-of-date carton data still feeds case requirements, so errors persist. Reconciling conflicting carton data delays finalizing case requirements. Detecting incomplete or conflicting source data requires data-management diligence and domain knowledge. Decisions on bad data cause expensive rework and requalification.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the specified carton count and orientation do not fit within shipping-case tolerances
rows.plain: Make sure the planned number and arrangement of cartons fit properly inside the shipping case. This helps prevent damage or packing problems during shipment.
rows.imp_rat: Count/orientation not fitting case tolerances stalls loading (relevance 7); consequences are jams and rework rather than escape (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Established pack-pattern software and prototype trials catch most misfits, but tolerance stack-ups still occasionally surface in production. Fit checks are done during design and trial runs, but iterating on carton count and orientation still consumes days of layout and test cycles. Determining fit within case tolerances relies on experienced packaging engineers using CAD or pallet-optimization tools plus manual judgement. Getting fit wrong drives rework, scrapped cases, and re-qualification costs that the status quo only partly prevents.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of carton groups whose arrangement differs from the specified case pattern
rows.plain: Keep the groups of boxes arranged in the required pattern. This helps the boxes fit, move, and be handled as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Arrangement differing from the specified pattern is relevant to correct loading (relevance 7); consequences are fit and closure rework, not typically escape (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Pattern forming plates and guides give consistent results on automated packers, but manual stations still show recurring arrangement variance. Correcting pattern deviations interrupts the loading rhythm and adds time. Matching the specified pattern demands operator recognition and care, especially with complex layouts. Pattern rework mostly costs labor rather than materials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that case-packing qualification activities compromise applicable sterile-area contamination controls
rows.plain: Make sure testing the case-packing process does not weaken controls that keep the sterile work area free from contamination. This protects the cleanliness of the area during testing.
rows.imp_rat: Qualification activities compromising sterile-area controls is peripheral to routine packing (relevance 5) but contamination of sterile areas is a severe safety event (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Sterile-area contamination controls are a mature, tightly enforced discipline, making this need well served. Contamination-control precautions during qualification are proceduralized and rarely delay the work. Executing qualification without breaching sterile-area controls requires disciplined trained personnel. A contamination breach is extremely costly, so investment here is prioritized.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that case-packing conditions drift beyond validated limits during production
rows.plain: Keep the conditions for packing cases within the tested acceptable range during production. This helps keep the process working as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Conditions drifting beyond validated limits during production risks out-of-control operation (relevance 6); can produce undetected defects (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Some parameters are monitored, but many case-packing conditions lack continuous alarms so drift can go unnoticed. Continuous monitoring is passive and adds no delay. Recognizing drift requires attentive interpretation of process signals. Undetected drift causes batch-scale waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shipping-case configurations lacking substantiated protection against applicable distribution hazards
rows.plain: Make sure every shipping package design has reliable evidence that it can protect the products from the problems they may face during delivery. This helps prevent damage in transit.
rows.imp_rat: Configurations lacking substantiated distribution-hazard protection is a validation-evidence gap (relevance 5); unsubstantiated protection can let field failures occur (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: ISTA-type qualification covers major formats but configuration changes often outpace substantiation. Distribution-hazard substantiation is upfront validation, not per-run. It requires specialized transport-simulation and packaging expertise. Unsubstantiated protection risks distribution loss costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of cartoned units being trapped or damaged during shipping-case closure
rows.plain: Prevent products from getting caught or damaged when packed cases are closed for shipping. This helps ensure the products arrive in good condition.
rows.imp_rat: Units trapped/damaged during closure affects the loading step (relevance 6); results in damaged product and rework, generally detectable (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Case sealers with guide rails largely prevent trapping, though manual closure still causes occasional carton damage. Clearing trapped cartons during closure adds unplanned time. Avoiding trapping flaps requires operator care when guiding cartons into the case. Trapped or damaged cartons waste product and packaging.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartoned units damaged during grouping or case loading
rows.plain: Prevent boxed products from being damaged while they are grouped together or loaded into shipping cases. This helps them arrive in good condition.
rows.imp_rat: Units damaged during grouping/case loading affects integrity of the loaded product (relevance 6); results in waste and possible escape (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Qualified equipment and adjustments keep damage low but variability persists across formats. Preventive setup adjustments are within normal changeover time. Minimizing damage depends on equipment tuning skill and monitoring. Damage during loading wastes finished goods.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartoned units loaded in orientations susceptible to distribution damage
rows.plain: Load boxed products in positions that protect them from damage during shipping. This helps keep them from being crushed, shifted, or otherwise harmed in transit.
rows.imp_rat: Units loaded in damage-susceptible orientations affects distribution survival (relevance 6); results in field damage, moderate consequence (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Fixtured loading and vision guidance control orientation well, though subtle susceptibilities can be missed at design time. Confirming orientation across a pattern adds modest inspection time. Identifying damage-susceptible orientations needs packaging and product-fragility knowledge. Wrong orientations drive damage costs that orientation-controlled loading partly prevents.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shipping cases exceeding allowable mass limits when loaded as specified
rows.plain: Keep shipping cases within the allowed weight when filled as planned. This helps make them safe to move and transport.
rows.imp_rat: Cases exceeding allowable mass when loaded as specified affects handling/compliance (relevance 6); creates injury and carrier-rejection risk (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Inline checkweighing is a mature, ubiquitous control that makes mass-limit breaches rare. Mass verification is near-instant with inline checkweighing. Ergonomic and regulatory mass limits are documented and easy to apply. Overweight cases risk carrier penalties and rejections that current weighing reliably avoids.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of loaded shipping cases exceeding applicable mass or dimensional constraints
rows.plain: Keep loaded shipping cases within the allowed weight and size limits. This helps make them safe and acceptable to handle and transport.
rows.imp_rat: Cases exceeding mass/dimensional constraints affect handling and compliance (relevance 6); consequences are handling injury risk and carrier rejection (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Checkweighers and dimensioners reliably flag out-of-spec cases, so violations are uncommon. Mass and dimension checks are quick against defined constraints. Applying documented mass/dimensional limits requires little specialized skill. Exceeding constraints triggers carrier surcharges and rework that checkweighing prevents.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which the risk assessment underrepresents worst-case carton, shipping-case, product, and environmental conditions
rows.plain: Make sure the risk review fully considers the most difficult expected conditions for cartons, shipping containers, products, and the surrounding environment. Otherwise, the process may seem safer on paper than it really is.
rows.imp_rat: Risk assessment underrepresenting worst-case conditions is a validation-adequacy gap (relevance 4); an underscoped assessment can allow field failures (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Risk-assessment practice exists but worst-case coverage is frequently incomplete, leaving latent failure modes. Building worst-case condition sets into risk assessments adds upfront planning time but is not the core loss here. Identifying true worst-case carton, case, product, and environmental combinations relies heavily on expert judgement. Underrepresented worst cases surface as costly field failures rather than upfront spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of carton misorientation disrupting group formation and case loading
rows.plain: Keep cartons from turning the wrong way often enough to interrupt sorting them into groups and loading them into shipping cases. This helps the packing line keep moving smoothly.
rows.imp_rat: Carton misorientation disrupting group formation impedes throughput of the job (relevance 6) but produces stoppages and jams that are recoverable (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Orientation guides help, but carton misorientation disrupting formation remains a common recurring nuisance. Misoriented cartons cause stoppages that consume time to clear and realign. Recognizing and correcting misorientation before it disrupts loading requires attentive operators. Disruptions cost throughput and occasionally damaged cartons.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the specified carton arrangement fails to fit because of carton or case dimensional variation
rows.plain: Make sure the planned carton arrangement will still fit even when cartons or cases vary slightly in size. This helps prevent packing problems.
rows.imp_rat: Dimensional variation preventing fit disrupts loading (relevance 6); it mainly causes jams and slowdowns that are recoverable (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Tolerance analysis and incoming-material specs help, but real-world corrugate and carton swelling still produces intermittent fit failures. Accounting for dimensional variation requires additional measurement and sampling that lengthens qualification. Interpreting carton and case dimensional variation demands statistical and materials knowledge that not every specifier has. Unaddressed variation causes jams and rejected cases, adding material waste that current practice under-controls.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in specified case dimensions, tolerances, carton orientation, and acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Make the requirements for case size, allowed size differences, carton direction, and passing or failing clear and specific. This helps suppliers and workers know exactly what the packaging must be like.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in specified dimensions/tolerances/criteria is a definitional issue that indirectly affects packing correctness (relevance 5); unclear criteria can propagate defects (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Spec templates help, but ambiguous or under-defined case criteria remain a common source of downstream error. Clarifying ambiguous dimensions and acceptance criteria consumes back-and-forth time. Writing unambiguous tolerances and acceptance criteria requires disciplined specification skill. Ambiguity causes misbuilt configurations and rework whose cost outweighs careful specification.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of packed lots presented for disposition with unresolved case-packing deviations
rows.plain: Reduce the number of packed groups of products sent for a release decision while packing problems are still unresolved. This helps prevent questionable product from moving forward.
rows.imp_rat: Lots presented for disposition with unresolved deviations is a workflow/quality-hygiene concern (relevance 5); unresolved deviations risk erroneous release decisions (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Review gates catch many, but lots still reach disposition with open deviations under schedule pressure. Resolving deviations before disposition extends lot review time. Judging resolution completeness requires quality expertise. Unresolved deviations at disposition drive hold costs and rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartoned units damaged during group formation
rows.plain: Avoid damaging boxes while grouping them together. This keeps the products protected and ready to ship.
rows.imp_rat: Units damaged during group formation affects product integrity at an early step (relevance 6); typically results in waste, detectable (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Collation systems and manual care keep damage during formation relatively low but not eliminated. Careful group formation adds marginal handling time. Avoiding damage during grouping depends on operator handling technique. Damaged units during grouping are scrapped, wasting product.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons crushed, scuffed, torn, or opened during case loading
rows.plain: Prevent boxes from being crushed, marked, torn, or opened while they are loaded into larger cases. This keeps the products’ packaging in good condition.
rows.imp_rat: Cartons crushed/scuffed/torn during loading affects presentation and integrity (relevance 6); mostly cosmetic/waste consequences (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Guides and controlled machinery limit damage, but manual loading still crushes or scuffs cartons at a recurring rate. Handling to avoid carton damage slows manual loading slightly. Gentle handling to prevent crushing and scuffing depends on operator technique. Damaged cartons are discarded, wasting finished product and packaging.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that personnel are injured while accessing or removing the source of a case-packer disruption
rows.plain: Protect people from injury when they reach the source of a case-packing problem or remove it. This allows the problem to be cleared safely and quickly.
rows.imp_rat: Personnel injury while clearing a disruption is peripheral to the packing job's output (relevance 3) but bodily harm to a worker is a severe consequence (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Machine guarding, interlocks and LOTO are mature, widely enforced practices that make injury during clearing uncommon. Lockout and safe-access procedures add time to interventions but that is accepted. Safe access depends on trained adherence to LOTO and guarding rules. Injuries are costly but the preventive measures themselves are inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of consequential stacking, vibration, shock, and environmental stresses omitted from the load-integrity evaluation
rows.plain: Make sure the load-safety review includes all important stresses from stacking, shaking, impacts, and surrounding conditions that could damage the load.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting stresses from load-integrity evaluation is a validation-design gap peripheral to routine packing (relevance 4); an inadequate evaluation can let distribution failures escape (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Standard test batteries cover common stresses, but scope decisions are inconsistent and consequential stresses do get omitted. Adding stress scenarios to the evaluation lengthens the test program. Knowing which stacking, vibration, shock, and environmental stresses to include requires distribution-engineering expertise. Omitted stresses surface as field failures whose cost dwarfs the testing that was skipped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percent of scheduled running period with case-packing output below target
rows.plain: Keep the case-packing line producing close to its expected amount for most of the time it is scheduled to run.
rows.imp_rat: Output below target is a throughput/efficiency concern (relevance 5) with cost consequences but no direct quality escape (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Case-packing rarely holds target across an entire shift because it accumulates the effects of every downstream fault, starvation and changeover, making sustained on-target running the exception. Throughput dips are tracked on shift boards and OEE reports but detecting a sustained below-target trend still lags real time by minutes to a full shift. Holding output at target depends heavily on an experienced overseer reading the line and reacting, so consistency varies with who is running the shift. Lost output is visible in production reports but the cost of chronic under-target running is often absorbed rather than actively recovered.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartoned units damaged during Case Packer blockage removal
rows.plain: Reduce damage to packaged units while workers remove a blockage from the machine that packs them into shipping cases. This helps prevent waste and rejected product.
rows.imp_rat: Units damaged during blockage removal affects product integrity during recovery (relevance 5); results in waste, generally detectable (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Damage during blockage removal recurs whenever clearance is rushed, giving inconsistent outcomes. Careful blockage removal takes longer than rushing it. Avoiding damage during blockage clearance requires operator technique. Units damaged during clearance are wasted product.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of prepared shipping cases that are malformed or not square when presented for loading
rows.plain: Make sure each shipping case is properly shaped and square before loading. This helps the cases fit, stack, and travel safely.
rows.imp_rat: Malformed/non-square cases presented for loading impede correct loading (relevance 6); typically cause jams/rework (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Case erectors produce mostly square cases, but malformed cases still appear at a recurring rate from worn blanks or setup drift. Reforming malformed cases interrupts loading and adds time. Recognizing and rejecting non-square cases relies on operator judgment. Malformed cases waste blanks and cause jams.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of prepared shipping cases incorrectly oriented or positioned for loading
rows.plain: Make sure each prepared shipping case is facing the right way and placed correctly before it is loaded. This helps loading go smoothly and prevents handling problems.
rows.imp_rat: Cases incorrectly oriented/positioned for loading disrupts the loading step (relevance 6); mainly causes jams/rework (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Case erectors present cases consistently, so orientation errors are relatively uncommon on automated lines. Repositioning misoriented cases adds handling time. Correct case orientation for loading depends on operator setup and attention. Misoriented cases cause rework at labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of shipping cases rejected for incorrectly grouped or loaded cartoned units during the shift
rows.plain: Reduce the number of shipping cases rejected because the packaged units were grouped or loaded incorrectly during a work shift. This helps the line meet its production goal.
rows.imp_rat: Cases rejected for grouping/loading errors reflect defects being caught (relevance 6); the reject cost is moderate and product is contained (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Pattern-forming mechanisms and end-of-line checks catch most grouping errors, but occasional miss-loads still slip through, giving moderate consistency. Rejects are usually caught at end-of-line inspection, adding rework time after the error has already been made. Correct grouping depends on machine setup plus operator vigilance, so defect rates track operator attention. Rejected cases waste materials and labor for teardown and repack, a recurring absorbed cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of cartoned-unit movement within shipping cases during distribution
rows.plain: Keep boxed units from shifting around inside shipping cases while they are being transported. This helps prevent damage and keeps the shipment organized.
rows.imp_rat: Cartoned-unit movement in cases during distribution affects protection (relevance 5); can cause damage but usually limited (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Void-fill and tight pack patterns are standard and effective, though residual shift during distribution persists in some configs. Tuning fill and dunnage to limit movement adds iteration time during design. Balancing snug fit against loadability takes experienced packaging judgement. Excess movement drives damage costs while over-packing wastes dunnage material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that carton or shipping-case characteristics exceed qualified input ranges during routine production
rows.plain: The boxes and shipping containers used in regular production should stay within the sizes and features that the process has been tested to handle. This helps keep the process safe and reliable.
rows.imp_rat: Inputs exceeding qualified ranges risks out-of-validation operation (relevance 5); consequences are defects if undetected, usually flagged (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Incoming inspection and specification control are standard, yet materials still occasionally exceed qualified ranges in production. Monitoring carton and case characteristics against qualified ranges is routine and adds little delay. Recognizing when incoming material drifts outside qualified ranges needs trained inspection but is well-supported. Out-of-range material driving rejects is a real but generally contained cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of carton jams during the first production run after changeover
rows.plain: Prevent boxes from getting stuck during the first production run after the equipment is changed to handle a different format. This helps the new run start smoothly.
rows.imp_rat: Carton jams in the first run after changeover disrupt throughput at a transition (relevance 5); cause stoppages/minor damage (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: First-run jams after changeover are a well-known recurring problem with only partial mitigation. Jams in the first run stop production and each clearance eats minutes.
