Preventing early jams depends on operator skill in dialing in the setup. Jams damage cartons and cases, adding scrap cost during the vulnerable startup window.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies between case-packer event records and upstream production records during validation
rows.plain: Make sure the case-packer's event records match the production records from earlier in the process during testing. This helps show that the equipment's reported performance is trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies between packer and upstream records during validation is a data-integrity/validation concern peripheral to physical packing (relevance 4); can undermine traceability (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Loose or timestamp-drifting interfaces between case-packer PLCs and MES/serialization layers routinely produce record mismatches that surface late, so this remains a recurring integrity gap. Reconciliation of case-packer event logs against upstream production records typically happens during validation windows that add days of review, but the delay itself is not the core loss here. Detecting discrepancies still leans heavily on validation engineers manually cross-referencing timestamped datasets, a judgement-intensive task poorly supported by automated comparison tooling. Discrepancy investigation consumes engineering hours and can trigger rework, but material cost is modest relative to the integrity risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of cartoned-unit jams during group formation
rows.plain: Reduce the number of cartons that get stuck while being arranged into groups. This helps the grouping work continue without interruptions.
rows.imp_rat: Unit jams during group formation disrupt throughput (relevance 5); cause stoppages and minor damage (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Collation jams recur intermittently and remain a common source of minor stoppages. Unit jams during group formation stop flow and take time to clear. Clearing collation jams requires operator know-how about the grouping mechanism. Jams cost throughput and occasionally damage units.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of carton or shipping-case jams during loading
rows.plain: Reduce the number of cartons or larger shipping boxes that get stuck while being loaded. This helps loading continue without stops.
rows.imp_rat: Carton/case jams during loading disrupt throughput (relevance 5); mostly cause stoppages and minor damage (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Jams remain a recurring, somewhat unpredictable interruption despite guide adjustments. Carton and case jams during loading directly halt the line and consume clearance time. Clearing jams safely and quickly requires operator familiarity with the machine. Jams waste throughput and can damage cartons during clearance.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of closed shipping cases reopened because carton quantity or orientation is incorrect
rows.plain: Reduce the number of sealed shipping boxes that must be reopened because they contain the wrong number of cartons or the cartons are facing the wrong way. This prevents wasted materials and extra work.
rows.imp_rat: Reopening cases for count/orientation is corrective rework (relevance 5); it catches errors so the consequence is cost/time, not escape (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: In-line checks before sealing reduce reopens, but manual stations still reopen cases with some regularity. Reopening closed cases is a significant time loss when quantity or orientation is wrong. Avoiding reopens depends on catching errors before closure, which requires vigilance. Reopening damages the case and wastes sealing material and labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of carton jams at the Case Packer infeed
rows.plain: Reduce the number of cartons that get stuck as they enter the machine that packs them into larger boxes. This helps the line keep moving.
rows.imp_rat: Carton jams at the infeed impede feeding into the job (relevance 5); mainly throughput stoppages (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Infeed jams are a recurring nuisance that guides and sensors only partly mitigate. Infeed jams stop feeding and require time to clear before packing resumes. Clearing infeed jams needs operator familiarity with feed adjustments. Infeed jams cost throughput and can damage cartons.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of misalignment among carton groups, open shipping cases, and the loading path
rows.plain: Keep the carton groups, open shipping cases, and loading route lined up correctly. This helps items move through the setup smoothly and prevents handling mistakes.
rows.imp_rat: Misalignment among groups, cases, and loading path affects transfer smoothness (relevance 5); consequences are jams and misloads, recoverable (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Alignment is set manually and drifts, so misalignment is a common source of early-run faults. Aligning carton groups, cases, and the loading path is an iterative adjustment that consumes setup time. Achieving alignment demands mechanical feel and experience with the station's transfer geometry. Misalignment causes jams that damage cartons and cases, driving material loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of sequencing mismatch between carton-group transfer and case presentation
rows.plain: Move carton groups in the same order and timing in which the shipping cases are presented. This helps prevent cartons from being placed in the wrong case or handled out of order.
rows.imp_rat: Sequencing mismatch between group transfer and case presentation disrupts loading flow (relevance 5); causes jams/misloads, recoverable (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Servo recipes hold timing reasonably once set, but re-establishing correct sequencing after changeover remains error-prone. Tuning the timing between transfer and case presentation requires trial cycles that extend startup. Sequencing adjustments rely on skilled reading of machine motion and servo timing. Mistimed transfers jam and crush product, generating scrap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of case-loading interruptions caused by the specified carton grouping or orientation
rows.plain: Reduce stops while loading larger boxes that are caused by the chosen carton grouping or direction. This helps the boxes be filled more efficiently.
rows.imp_rat: Loading interruptions caused by specified grouping/orientation disrupts throughput (relevance 5); mainly stoppages, recoverable (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Debugging and trial runs reduce jams, but grouping-induced interruptions still recur in production. Loading interruptions from a chosen grouping directly reduce uptime and are only found in running. Diagnosing which grouping causes stoppages requires line-troubleshooting experience. Interruptions cut throughput and raise labor cost per case.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the specified grouping and loading arrangement cannot sustain the required case-packing pace
rows.plain: Make sure the planned grouping and arrangement of packages can keep up with the required packing speed. Otherwise, the packing process may fall behind.
rows.imp_rat: Arrangement unable to sustain required pace is a throughput-design concern (relevance 5); consequences are slowdowns, not escapes (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Rate trials and OEE data give evidence, but pace shortfalls of new configurations still emerge in ramp-up. Whether an arrangement sustains line pace is only proven late in commissioning, causing throughput surprises. Predicting speed feasibility for a given grouping requires seasoned line-integration expertise. An arrangement that can't hold pace forces slow-downs or capital changes that hurt cost per unit.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of loading interruptions caused by the proposed case-loading configuration
rows.plain: Reduce stops while loading larger boxes that are caused by the proposed loading setup. This helps confirm that the setup will work reliably.
rows.imp_rat: Loading interruptions from the proposed configuration is a throughput/design concern (relevance 5); consequences are stoppages, recoverable (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: FAT/SAT trials catch many issues, yet the proposed configuration can still interrupt loading in live conditions. Configuration-driven stoppages waste production time and appear during commissioning. Isolating configuration causes of interruptions needs mechanical and integration know-how. Repeated interruptions increase operating cost and lower yield.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of failed or incorrect case-packer responses to upstream line-control states during validation
rows.plain: Reduce how often the case-packing machine responds incorrectly when the equipment feeding it changes state during testing. This helps show that the machine will work reliably in real production.
rows.imp_rat: Failed responses to line-control states during validation is a validation-scope concern peripheral to routine output (relevance 4); indicates control gaps (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Standard FAT/SAT and integration test protocols exercise nominal line-control handshakes fairly well, yet intermittent or race-condition failures still slip through, leaving a real residual gap. Testing all line-control state transitions extends validation duration, though timing is secondary to whether the responses are correct. Verifying correct handshake behavior across start/stop/fault states demands controls expertise that is scarce and error-prone when scripting edge cases. Failed responses caught late can force revalidation cycles that burn budget, but direct material loss is limited.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of the same case-packer disruption recurring after restart
rows.plain: Reduce how often the case-packing machine has the same problem again after it is restarted. This helps prevent repeated interruptions to production.
rows.imp_rat: Same disruption recurring after restart is a reliability/troubleshooting concern peripheral to correctness (relevance 4); causes repeated downtime, recoverable (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Status-quo firefighting restores running but seldom eliminates the cause, so the same disruptions recur. Recurring faults repeatedly consume response time because root cause is rarely closed out promptly. Preventing recurrence needs root-cause analysis skill often unavailable at shift level. Repeat faults multiply downtime and repair cost over time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartoned units discarded after damage during grouping or case loading
rows.plain: Reduce the number of boxed products thrown away because they are damaged while being grouped or loaded into larger cases. This saves usable products and materials.
rows.imp_rat: Units discarded after damage is a waste/cost metric peripheral to correct packing (relevance 4); consequence is material loss, contained (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Careful handling and guides limit discards, but a recurring level of damage-driven scrap persists. Handling discarded damaged units adds minor time. Minimizing damage-related discards depends on operator handling skill. Each discarded unit is finished-product waste directly hitting yield.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required format parts are missing or damaged when changeover starts
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that needed parts for the new package type are missing or damaged when the machine is changed over.
rows.imp_rat: Missing/damaged format parts at changeover is a readiness/logistics issue peripheral to correct packing (relevance 4); causes delay, easily absorbed (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Change-part kitting helps but incomplete kits and undetected wear mean parts are frequently found missing or damaged only when needed. Discovering missing or damaged parts at changeover start halts progress while replacements are located. Identifying the correct change parts and spotting damage relies on operator knowledge of the format kit. Damaged parts and emergency sourcing carry direct replacement and downtime costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of production stoppages caused by case-packer faults during the shift
rows.plain: Reduce production stoppages caused by faults in the machine that packs boxes during a work shift. This helps the operation meet its planned output.
rows.imp_rat: Stoppages from case-packer faults reduce availability (relevance 4) with cost/schedule impact but no quality escape (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Case-packer faults recur with predictable frequency and current practice reacts rather than prevents, so stoppage duration stays inconsistent. Fault stoppages are common and clearing them relies on operator reaction plus maintenance response, so downtime durations remain long and variable. Fast recovery depends on scarce troubleshooting expertise, and inexperienced crews extend stoppages while diagnosing the fault. Each stoppage burns labor and lost throughput, and status-quo tools do little to contain that cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of conflict among case-packing acceptance criteria used by quality, engineering, validation, and production
rows.plain: Use one consistent set of packing rules instead of having different teams apply rules that conflict with one another.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting acceptance criteria across functions is a governance/administrative issue peripheral to the physical packing job (relevance 4) with moderate, correctable consequences (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Cross-functional criteria review boards exist but conflicts still routinely surface late, so consistency is only partially assured. Reconciling conflicting acceptance criteria across four functions typically requires extended review cycles that the status quo does little to shorten. Aligning quality, engineering, validation, and production criteria demands scarce cross-functional judgement that is hard to standardize. Rework driven by criteria conflicts consumes engineering and quality hours but is rarely tracked as a distinct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of case-packing stoppages caused by packaging material depletion before batch completion
rows.imp_rat: Stoppages from material depletion before batch completion is a throughput/supply issue peripheral to correctness (relevance 4); causes delay (severity 3).
rows.sat_rat: Replenishment routines reduce depletion stoppages, but they still recur when monitoring lapses. Material depletion mid-batch stops packing and costs replenishment time. Avoiding depletion requires operator monitoring of consumables. Stoppages waste throughput.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between incoming carton flow and achievable case-packing pace
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between incoming flow and achievable pace is a throughput-balancing issue peripheral to correctness (relevance 4); mainly efficiency loss (severity 3).
rows.sat_rat: Buffering and line control help, but pace mismatches remain a recurring source of interruption. Mismatched infeed and packing pace causes backups or starvation that waste time. Balancing pace requires operator adjustment and line coordination. Pace mismatch reduces throughput efficiency.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of shipping cases being unavailable when completed carton groups are ready for loading
rows.imp_rat: Cases unavailable when groups are ready is a supply-timing/throughput issue peripheral to correctness (relevance 4); causes idle time (severity 3).
rows.sat_rat: Case magazines and buffers usually keep cases available, so shortages at the loading moment are relatively infrequent. Case unavailability stalls loading until cases are supplied, costing time. Keeping cases available requires operator anticipation of supply needs. Stalls waste labor and throughput.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of additional station adjustments required before uninterrupted case packing begins
rows.imp_rat: Additional station adjustments before uninterrupted packing is a setup-efficiency issue peripheral to correctness (relevance 4); causes minor delay (severity 3).
rows.sat_rat: Repeated tweaking reflects an unstable setup process rather than a confident one. Extra adjustment iterations before smooth running directly extend changeover duration. The number of adjustments needed depends on how expertly the initial setup was done. Each additional adjustment consumes product and material during trial runs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the case-packer disruption source is misidentified before a response is authorized
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying the disruption source before response is a troubleshooting/diagnostic issue peripheral to packing correctness (relevance 3); causes rework and possibly unsafe action (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Without good fault data operators often misattribute the source, so correct first-time diagnosis is inconsistent. Misdiagnosis extends response time as crews chase the wrong cause. Correct fault identification is squarely a diagnostic-expertise problem and is the main dependence on scarce troubleshooting skill. Wrong-cause responses waste labor and parts on ineffective fixes.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of cartoned-unit starvation at the case packer during the shift
rows.imp_rat: Cartoned-unit starvation is a throughput-flow issue peripheral to correct packing (relevance 4); causes idle time, minor consequences (severity 3).
rows.sat_rat: Accumulation conveyors and infeed buffers dampen many short interruptions, so full starvation stoppages are moderated though not eliminated. Upstream starvation is usually visible via accumulation sensors but buffering and operator response only partially shorten the idle windows. Anticipating starvation before it stops the packer takes practiced line-balancing judgement that not all operators possess. Starvation idles staffed equipment, wasting labor and capacity with little status-quo mechanism to recover it.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of shipping-case supply interruptions during the shift
rows.imp_rat: Shipping-case supply interruptions are a throughput/logistics issue peripheral to correctness (relevance 4); cause idle time (severity 3).
rows.sat_rat: Magazine capacity and low-level alarms make blank supply fairly predictable, so interruptions are usually short and avoidable. Case-blank supply interruptions cause immediate stops, and replenishment time depends on operator restocking cadence. Restocking blanks is a low-skill task but anticipating runout takes routine attention. Interruptions idle the line with modest direct material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of blocked completed-case discharge during the shift
rows.imp_rat: Blocked completed-case discharge is a throughput/flow issue peripheral to correctness (relevance 4); causes delay, easily recovered (severity 3).
rows.sat_rat: Outfeed sensors and case-conveyor accumulation catch most blockages reliably, but downstream palletizer stalls still cause recurring backups. Blocked discharge is quickly signalled by a full outfeed but clearing downstream congestion still idles the packer for the duration. Resolving discharge blockage is largely manual clearing requiring little specialized skill. The blockage wastes staffed capacity while it persists, an absorbed throughput cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required for case-packing output to return to target after planned changeovers
rows.imp_rat: Time to return to target after changeover is a throughput-recovery metric peripheral to correctness (relevance 4); efficiency-only consequence (severity 3).
rows.sat_rat: Changeover procedures give a fairly repeatable ramp, but first-off adjustments still cause variable settling times. Return-to-target after changeover is a well-known ramp period that current SMED-style routines shorten but do not eliminate. Fast, stable restart after changeover leans on experienced setters, so ramp time varies with crew skill. Extended ramps waste capacity and generate startup scrap, a recognized recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of case-packing holds awaiting quality or documentation disposition during the shift
rows.imp_rat: Holds awaiting quality/documentation disposition are a delay/administrative concern (relevance 4); consequence is schedule impact, product contained (severity 3).
rows.sat_rat: Hold resolution depends on human availability and paperwork, making the duration erratic and often longer than needed. Quality/documentation holds can stall cases for extended periods while dispositions are chased manually. Resolving holds requires quality-authority judgement that is not always immediately on hand. Held cases tie up WIP and staff time awaiting sign-off.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of unexplained variation in grouping, loading, damage, and rejection results across equivalent qualification runs
rows.imp_rat: Unexplained variation across qualification runs is a validation-analysis concern peripheral to routine packing (relevance 3); indirect consequences (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Statistical acceptance and replicate runs help, but residual unexplained variation remains a recurring headache in case-packing qualification. Investigating unexplained run-to-run variation lengthens qualification timelines with repeated reruns. Diagnosing subtle sources of variation across equivalent runs requires seasoned validation expertise that is often in short supply. Unexplained variation drives extra runs and material consumption that inflate qualification cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of staging unreleased or expired packaging materials at the case packer
rows.imp_rat: Staging unreleased/expired materials risks non-conforming packaging; relevant and consequential but held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Status labeling and quarantine controls make expired-material staging fairly uncommon. Checking release/expiry status adds brief staging time. Verifying status requires operator access to release information. Using unreleased or expired material triggers costly holds.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of prior-lot material remaining at the station when the next lot begins
rows.imp_rat: Prior-lot material remaining at changeover risks mix-up; moderately relevant to loading job and serious, but capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Line clearance procedures reduce carryover, but residual prior-lot material remains a recurring risk. Clearing prior-lot material adds changeover time. Thorough line clearance depends on operator diligence. Leftover prior-lot material causes costly mix-ups.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of damaged or visibly contaminated packaging materials that pass verification
rows.imp_rat: Damaged/contaminated materials passing verification undermines packaging quality; relevant and consequential but held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Manual inspection catches most gross damage but subtle defects recurrently slip through. Inspecting materials for damage adds brief verification time. Detecting damaged or contaminated materials relies on operator visual inspection. Passing damaged materials wastes downstream materials and effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a product or label mix-up when case packing resumes
rows.imp_rat: Product/label mix-up on resume is a serious identity error relevant to grouping; capped at 3.0 per ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Line clearance and label checks reduce mix-ups but manual verification still leaves residual risk. Verifying product and label identity at resume adds a short confirmation step. Correct identity confirmation depends on operator diligence in reading labels and codes. A mix-up causes costly recalls and rework.

rows.source_job: Verify pack quality before run release
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a weak or incomplete case closure passing pre-release verification
rows.imp_rat: Weak/incomplete closure passing verification risks case failure in transit; relevant to protection, capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Visual seal checks miss weak-but-intact closures, so incomplete closures still pass verification. Checking closure quality at pre-release is quick per sample. Assessing seal strength by eye demands experience. Weak closures cause case failure and product loss downstream.

rows.source_job: Verify pack quality before run release
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an incorrect carton quantity or arrangement passing pre-release verification
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect carton quantity/arrangement passing pre-release verification is a core grouping defect; serious but capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Sampling-based pre-release checks catch gross errors but intermittent count/arrangement faults still pass. Pre-release verification of count and arrangement adds a defined check step. Reliable verification depends on the checker's attentiveness and standard knowledge. Missed errors at release lead to downstream rework and chargebacks.

rows.source_job: Verify pack quality before run release
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of damaged cartoned units remaining concealed in accepted shipping cases
rows.imp_rat: Damaged units concealed in accepted cases is a hidden quality defect relevant to loading; serious but capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Once cases are sealed there is little means to detect internal damage, making this poorly controlled. Concealed damage isn't caught in normal timing since cases are already closed. Detecting hidden damage is very hard even for experienced operators. Concealed damaged units reach customers, causing complaints and returns.

rows.source_job: Verify pack quality before run release
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a deformed shipping case being accepted as meeting integrity requirements
rows.imp_rat: Deformed case accepted as meeting integrity undermines protection function; relevant and consequential but held at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Manual integrity inspection is subjective, so deformed cases occasionally pass acceptance. Judging case integrity is a fast visual check per sample. Recognizing a deformed-but-passing case requires trained judgement. Accepting deformed cases risks downstream collapse and product loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a safeguard remains bypassed when case packing resumes
rows.imp_rat: Safeguard remaining bypassed at resume is a safety/quality risk relevant to controlled operation; serious but held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Interlock status is often visible on the HMI, yet temporary bypasses left in place remain a known safety gap. Confirming no safeguard is bypassed is a fast check but sometimes overlooked at resume. Knowing which safeguards may have been bypassed during setup requires trained awareness. A bypassed safeguard rarely costs material but carries severe safety liability.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that cartons or cases from a prior batch remain when readiness is declared
rows.imp_rat: Prior-batch cartons remaining at readiness is a mix-up risk relevant to grouping; serious but capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Line-clearance checklists are standard and reasonably effective, though hidden residual units still occasionally remain. Clearing prior-batch cartons and cases is a quick sweep but easy to rush. Line clearance is procedural and needs little specialized skill, mostly diligence. Residual material causes mix-ups and rework rather than large direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the number of shipping cases whose recorded batch differs from the batch of their cartoned units
rows.imp_rat: Batch-mismatch in records is a traceability defect with recall implications; kept at ceiling per batch-2 ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Batch-code printing and scan checks catch most errors, but changeover transitions remain a recurring vulnerability. Batch mismatches surface at reconciliation rather than in real time. Correct batch tagging relies on operator changeover discipline. A wrong batch label can force rejection of good cases, a real waste and compliance risk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the number of shipping cases containing cartoned units from different batches
rows.imp_rat: Mixed-batch cases pose contamination/recall risk, relevant to correct grouping; consequences serious but capped at 3.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Line-clearance procedures at batch changes are standard but transition periods still allow occasional mixing. Mixed-batch cases are typically detected only during batch reconciliation. Preventing mixing depends on disciplined line clearance between batches. A mixed-batch case can invalidate traceability and force costly segregation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the number of shipping cases whose recorded composition differs from the cartoned units loaded
rows.imp_rat: Recorded-composition mismatch affects traceability integrity of loaded cases; relevant and consequential but held at 3.0 ceiling per ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Where barcode/scan verification is deployed record-to-content accuracy is fairly high, though manual steps still introduce occasional mismatches. Composition mismatches are usually found only during later reconciliation, well after packing. Accurate recording depends on correct manual entry or scanning discipline. Mismatches trigger investigation and potential recall exposure, a significant latent cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the number of shipping cases with no batch-record linkage
rows.imp_rat: Cases with no batch-record linkage breaks traceability; relevant to compliance of the job, capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: MES/batch-record systems auto-link most cases where deployed, so orphaned cases are relatively rare. Missing linkage is discovered during audits long after packing. Ensuring linkage depends on consistent record-keeping discipline. An unlinked case may be unsaleable, a direct loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the number of shipping cases released before traceability verification is complete
rows.imp_rat: Releasing cases before traceability verification risks uncontrolled shipment; relevant and consequential, held at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Release-gate controls are common but under pressure cases are sometimes released ahead of full verification. Traceability verification before release can add hold time when done properly. Judging verification completeness requires QA oversight. Early release risks recall and rework costs far exceeding the verification effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of cartoned-unit movement or compression within shipping cases during distribution
rows.imp_rat: Cartoned-unit movement/compression risks distribution damage, central to protection; consequences moderate-high but capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Validated fit and dunnage reduce movement, though loose patterns and variation still allow shifting. Void-fill and fit setup is part of normal changeover. Controlling movement depends on pack-design and setup judgement. Excess movement causes damage and product loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of shipping cases with unresolved carton-to-case or batch traceability gaps
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved carton-to-case/batch traceability gaps is a compliance defect relevant to the job; capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Serialization and batch records support traceability, but carton-to-case linkage gaps still surface. Traceability reconciliation adds record-review time. Resolving traceability gaps relies on record-keeping rigor. Gaps trigger costly investigations and hold time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that case packing resumes before authorized restart conditions are verified
rows.imp_rat: Resuming before authorized restart conditions verified risks uncontrolled output; relevant and consequential, capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Restart-authorization steps exist but are inconsistently enforced after brief stoppages. Restart verification adds brief hold time before resuming. Verifying restart conditions relies on supervisor judgement. Improper restart causes nonconforming output waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unresolved doubt that sealed cases will protect cartoned units through handling and distribution
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved doubt over case protection touches core protection function; serious but capped at 3.0 per ranking.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of shipping cases containing mixed product identities, lots, or disposition statuses
rows.imp_rat: Mixed product identities/lots/disposition in a case is a serious mix-up error; relevant to grouping, capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Line clearance and single-lot rules are standard and make mixed-identity cases rare when followed. Preventing mixed identities relies on line clearance that takes setup time. Enforcing segregation requires disciplined procedural adherence more than deep skill. Mixed lots cause expensive segregation, holds, or scrap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between the specified carton grouping and approved packaging specifications
rows.imp_rat: Deviation from approved packaging specs is moderately relevant to correct grouping, and consequences are real but capped by ranking; scored at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Change control and spec sign-off constrain deviation well in regulated settings, keeping mismatches fairly rare. Comparing grouping against approved specs adds review time. Detecting deviation from approved packaging specs requires document-control familiarity. Deviations cause nonconformances and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shipping cases whose traceability records do not match the physically loaded cartons
rows.imp_rat: Traceability records not matching physical cartons is a compliance defect; relevant and consequential but held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Automated aggregation strongly reduces mismatches, though manual interventions still create occasional discrepancies. Reconciling traceability records with physical loads adds verification time. Managing aggregation integrity needs specialized track-and-trace skill. Record-to-physical mismatches trigger costly investigations and holds.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that residual cartons or prior-batch materials cause a product mix-up during a configuration switch
rows.imp_rat: Residual cartons causing product mix-up during switch is a contamination/mix-up risk; serious but held at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Documented line-clearance procedures control this well, though human lapses during switchovers still occur. Clearance during configuration switches adds changeover time. Thorough clearance depends on operator diligence rather than advanced skill. A residual-carton mix-up can cause recalls that dwarf clearance cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an incorrect grouping or loading configuration is applied after a format switch
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect grouping/loading config applied after switch is a core defect; relevant and consequential but held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe management and setup checks reduce misapplication, but changeover errors still occur without full automation. Verification after format switch adds restart time. Applying the correct configuration relies on operator/setup competence and recipe systems. A wrong configuration causes scrap and downtime costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of case volume left unused by the specified carton arrangement
rows.imp_rat: Unused case volume is an efficiency/cost-optimization concern peripheral to correct packing (relevance 3); trivial consequence (severity 3).
rows.sat_rat: Cube-optimization software gives consistent patterns, but practical constraints leave predictable void. Optimizing fill efficiency adds design iteration time. Maximizing cube utilization requires pattern-optimization expertise and tools. Unused case volume wastes corrugate and freight, a direct and recurring cost the status quo only partly minimizes.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that critical grouping and case-loading failure modes remain unidentified under intended production conditions
rows.imp_rat: Unidentified failure modes under production conditions threaten reliable loading; relevant and consequential but held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: FMEA practice is common but critical failure modes still slip through under real production conditions. Thorough failure-mode identification extends planning time but that is not the primary loss. Surfacing all critical grouping and loading failure modes depends on deep expert insight into the equipment. Unidentified failure modes translate into downstream defect costs rather than upfront spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartoned units lacking a shipping-case parent after case completion
rows.imp_rat: Cartoned units lacking a case parent is a traceability aggregation defect; relevant and consequential but capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Modern aggregation systems enforce commissioning checks that reliably flag units without a case parent, so this is comparatively well handled though partial-case restarts still create occasional orphans. rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required packing positions assigned to unqualified operators
rows.imp_rat: Positions assigned to unqualified operators is a training/compliance issue, peripheral to physical job; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Skills matrices exist but under staffing pressure unqualified operators are still assigned, so adherence is imperfect. Verifying qualification before assignment adds little delay. This need is fundamentally about matching scarce qualified operators to positions, the core skill-supply constraint. Unqualified assignment causes defects and rework, an avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of required packing positions remaining uncovered after unplanned absences
rows.imp_rat: Uncovered positions after absences is a staffing delay, peripheral to loading function; consequences are throughput, capped within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Cover depends on ad-hoc call-ins so coverage speed and quality are inconsistent. Covering an unplanned absence takes time to locate and deploy a qualified replacement, leaving positions open. Replacements must be qualified, and the qualified pool is often thin, constraining fast cover. Uncovered positions either idle the line or force overtime, both costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time staffing remains mismatched after a packing-workload change
rows.imp_rat: Staffing mismatch after workload change is a capacity-timing delay, peripheral; recoverable consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Adjustments happen eventually but the lag and its length are inconsistent shift to shift. Rebalancing staff after a workload change is slow because reassignment is manual and reactive. Effective reassignment requires supervisory judgement about who can move where. The mismatch period wastes labor or loses output until corrected.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of required packing positions remaining uncovered during breaks and shift handoffs
rows.imp_rat: Positions uncovered during breaks/handoffs is a staffing delay, peripheral to loading function; recoverable consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Staggered-break and handover routines are standard practice and cover most gaps reliably. Break and handoff gaps are predictable and usually bridged by rotation schedules, so uncovered time is short. Rotation relies on cross-trained relief operators being available. Relief staffing carries modest labor cost that is normally planned for.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of mid-shift operator reassignments caused by initial staffing mismatches
rows.imp_rat: Mid-shift reassignments from staffing mismatch is rework/capacity, peripheral to loading job; recoverable consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Because initial plans are often rough, reactive reassignments recur predictably. Mid-shift reassignments disrupt flow and cost setup time each time they occur. They stem from imperfect initial skill/workload matching, a planning-judgement shortfall. Churn wastes labor and productivity with each move.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of case-packing changes implemented without a resolved validation impact
rows.imp_rat: Changes without resolved validation impact is administrative/procedural, peripheral to the physical loading job; consequences moderate and capped within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control systems catch most, but informal or minor changes sometimes bypass validation-impact resolution. Change-control review before implementation adds lead time. Assessing validation impact requires specialist judgement. Unvalidated changes risk costly nonconformance.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that validation-impacting case-packing deviations remain open without substantiated disposition
rows.imp_rat: Deviations open without disposition is a delay/compliance issue, peripheral to physical loading; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Tracking systems exist but aging open deviations remain a persistent problem. Open validation-impacting deviations frequently linger without timely disposition. Timely closure depends on scarce investigator and SME availability. Prolonged open deviations extend holds and carry inventory cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unexplained cartoned-unit reconciliation discrepancies at lot closure
rows.imp_rat: Unexplained reconciliation discrepancies at lot closure is a records ambiguity, peripheral to physical job; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Count reconciliation catches most, but small unexplained variances persist and require investigation. Reconciliation at lot closure is a defined time step. Explaining discrepancies requires investigative attention. Unexplained discrepancies force costly rework and holds.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of conflicting release-criteria interpretations for the same packed-lot evidence
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting release-criteria interpretations is a procedural ambiguity, peripheral; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Written criteria help, but ambiguous cases still produce divergent interpretations across reviewers. Resolving interpretation conflicts adds review delay. Consistent interpretation depends heavily on scarce senior QA judgement. Conflicting interpretations cause rework and inconsistent decisions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required case-packing records that are incomplete, missing, or illegible when reviewed
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete/illegible records at review is a documentation defect, peripheral to loading; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Good documentation practices are enforced, yet illegible or incomplete records remain a common review finding. Chasing incomplete records lengthens review time. Legibility and completeness depend on operator recording habits. Incomplete records force costly re-review and investigation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved case-count discrepancies across packaging and inventory records
rows.imp_rat: Case-count discrepancies across records is an inventory-reconciliation issue, peripheral; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: System reconciliation resolves most case-count discrepancies, with residual mismatches between packaging and inventory records. Cross-record reconciliation adds closure time. Resolving count conflicts relies on record-management diligence. Discrepancies drive investigation labor and inventory write-offs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable market requirements omitted from the packaging configuration criteria
rows.imp_rat: Market requirements omitted from config criteria is a specification-completeness issue, peripheral to physical job; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Regulatory intelligence exists but requirements are fragmented, so omissions in configuration criteria persist. Gathering all market requirements upfront lengthens criteria development. Knowing all applicable market/regulatory packaging requirements demands regulatory-affairs expertise. Missed market requirements cause rejected shipments and rework abroad.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required packaging configurations and distribution conditions excluded from compliance evidence
rows.imp_rat: Configurations excluded from compliance evidence is a coverage gap, indirectly relevant; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Compliance documentation practices exist but coverage gaps for distribution conditions recur. Assembling compliance evidence across configurations is time-consuming. Determining which configurations and conditions need evidence needs compliance expertise. Excluded configurations cause audit findings and remediation costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of evaluation criteria lacking objective acceptance thresholds
rows.imp_rat: Criteria lacking objective thresholds is a definition ambiguity, indirectly relevant; consequences moderate and capped within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Many criteria still lack objective acceptance thresholds, so pass/fail judgement varies. Defining objective thresholds for each criterion adds specification time. Converting subjective criteria into measurable thresholds needs metrology and standards expertise. Vague thresholds cause disputes and rework that objective limits would avoid.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of mismatch between standardized criteria and expected carton, case-material, and pack-pattern variation
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch of standardized criteria to variation is a definition/standards gap, peripheral to physical job; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standardization efforts exist but often lag real-world variation, leaving recurring criteria mismatches. Aligning standardized criteria to expected variation takes upfront analysis time. Anticipating material and pattern variation requires broad packaging experience. Mismatched criteria cause false rejects or missed defects, both costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of expected carton, case, and line conditions omitted from validation trials
rows.imp_rat: Conditions omitted from validation trials is a coverage gap, indirectly relevant; consequences contained, capped within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Validation protocols exist but rarely span all carton/case/line conditions, so gaps remain common. Broadening validation trials to cover more conditions extends validation timelines. Selecting the full range of conditions to trial requires strong DOE and packaging expertise. Omitted conditions produce field failures costlier than the added trials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in acceptance criteria for the proposed case-loading configuration
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in acceptance criteria for loading config is a definition issue, peripheral; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Templates help but acceptance criteria for new configurations still carry interpretive ambiguity. Removing ambiguity in acceptance criteria takes review and negotiation time. Writing unambiguous acceptance criteria requires disciplined technical-writing skill. Ambiguous criteria cause rework and disputes with real cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that upstream or downstream line disturbances are misattributed to the case packer during risk assessment
rows.imp_rat: Misattributing line disturbances to the case packer is an assessment-accuracy issue, peripheral to physical job; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Root-cause practices exist but cross-unit misattribution remains common and poorly controlled. Disentangling line disturbances during risk assessment can add investigation time. Correctly attributing disturbances to upstream/downstream versus the case packer takes strong diagnostic skill. Misattribution wastes engineering effort chasing the wrong unit.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that planned samples and repeated runs are insufficient to substantiate reproducible case packing
rows.imp_rat: Insufficient samples/runs to substantiate reproducibility is a validation-rigor issue, peripheral; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Sampling plans are standardized but are still sometimes too thin to substantiate reproducibility. Sizing sample plans and repeated runs is routine and does not drive delay. Determining statistically sufficient sampling requires statistical competence not always resident on the floor. Insufficient sampling leads to reruns and re-qualification costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that reproducible case packing remains unsubstantiated because repeated qualification evidence is insufficient
rows.imp_rat: Reproducibility unsubstantiated from insufficient evidence is a validation-rigor issue, peripheral; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Replicate-run practice exists but reproducibility often remains under-substantiated, a recurring gap. Repeating qualification to substantiate reproducibility extends timelines when evidence is thin. Judging whether repeated evidence is sufficient requires validation expertise. Insufficient repeated evidence forces costly additional runs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that qualification source evidence is unavailable during internal review, supplier evaluation, or regulatory inspection
rows.imp_rat: Qualification evidence unavailable at inspection is a records-availability issue, peripheral to physical job; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Document management systems make source evidence generally available, so this need is reasonably well served. Retrieving qualification evidence during inspection can be slow when records are dispersed. Locating and interpreting source evidence needs documentation familiarity more than deep expertise. Missing evidence during inspection risks findings whose remediation is costly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that periodic review fails to identify adverse trends, recurring deviations, or changes affecting the case-packer qualified state
rows.imp_rat: Periodic review missing adverse trends is an oversight process issue, peripheral to physical job; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Periodic review and trending programs are established, though they inconsistently catch slow adverse trends. Periodic review is a scheduled activity that does not itself impose urgent time pressure. Spotting adverse trends and subtle changes in qualified state requires analytical review skill. Missed trends lead to costly drift but review itself is relatively inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that case-packer claims are accepted under conditions unrepresentative of sterile fill-finish production
rows.imp_rat: Claims accepted under unrepresentative conditions is a qualification-rigor issue, peripheral to physical job; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Claims are often accepted on convenient rather than representative conditions, so confidence in real-world performance is uncertain and inconsistently governed. Setting up representative sterile fill-finish conditions for testing takes time to arrange. Judging whether test conditions truly represent sterile production requires deep process and regulatory expertise that few possess, making this heavily skill-bound. Representative testing consumes sterile-grade materials and constrained resources at notable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required carton formats, line states, and production scenarios omitted from validation
rows.imp_rat: Formats/scenarios omitted from validation is a coverage gap, indirectly relevant; consequences contained, capped within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Coverage gaps mean untested conditions may fail in production, but the root issue is the difficulty of scoping complete test matrices. Building broad validation coverage across formats and scenarios lengthens the validation program considerably. Deciding which carton formats, line states and scenarios must be tested is a judgement-heavy task depending on experienced validation engineers, and omissions stem directly from that scarce expertise. Extra scenarios cost test materials and labor, a budgeted but real expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the rate of cartoned-unit grouping and case loading below the claimed output under representative product mix
rows.imp_rat: Grouping/loading below claimed output is a throughput-shortfall reliability issue, moderately relevant; recoverable consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Performance qualification runs test rate reasonably well for nominal products, but representative mixed-product output is often under-tested so shortfalls appear later, leaving a moderate gap. Throughput shortfalls under representative product mix are the essence of this need, but the loss is missed rated output rather than pure delay. Assessing achievable rate across product mix requires performance-qualification expertise to design and interpret runs. Underperformance against claimed output erodes capacity value, an indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between shipping-case completion and record availability to upstream line control
rows.imp_rat: Delay in record availability to line control is a data-timing issue, peripheral to physical loading; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Message queues occasionally back up or drop, but data does become available upstream consistently under normal operation. The core need is latency between case completion and record availability upstream, which real-time or near-real-time messaging in current systems handles reasonably but not instantly. Once interfaces are configured, minimal operator skill is needed to maintain the data push. Latency imposes little direct material or money cost beyond occasional queuing delays.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of aggregation events recorded outside their actual packaging order
rows.imp_rat: Aggregation events recorded out of order is a data-sequencing issue, peripheral to physical job; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Buffering, asynchronous messaging and timestamp drift regularly record events out of their true packaging order, and this is not robustly guarded against in typical deployments. Event ordering is captured in real time so delay is not the issue. Ensuring correct event sequencing under buffering requires careful controls configuration that is error-prone. Out-of-order events rarely cause direct material loss but can invalidate audit trails.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that qualification-critical line-control or data-flow risks remain undiscovered until recommendation review
rows.imp_rat: Qualification risks undiscovered until review is a process-timing issue, peripheral to physical job; moderate consequences within ceiling.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of uncertainty about whether unit-to-case traceability is complete at compliance sign-off
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about traceability completeness at sign-off is an ambiguity, indirectly relevant; consequences contained, capped within ceiling.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator injury from unexpected Case Packer movement during blockage clearance
rows.imp_rat: Operator injury from unexpected movement during blockage clearance carries genuine safety severity, but it is peripheral to the packing CFJ and the batch ceiling caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Guarding, interlocks and lockout/tagout make injury from unexpected movement relatively well controlled. Lockout procedures add time to blockage clearance. Safe clearance depends on operator following lockout discipline. Injuries carry high human and financial cost, so controls are enforced.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator pinch or crush injury during case-loading intervention
rows.imp_rat: Pinch/crush injury during case-loading intervention has real safety severity but is peripheral to the grouping/loading CFJ and the ceiling caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Guarding and safety procedures make pinch/crush injuries relatively uncommon, though intervention risk persists. Safe intervention practices add minor time. Avoiding pinch/crush injury depends on operator adherence to safe methods. Injuries are costly, driving strong controls.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of active tallies being assigned to the wrong shipping case during case transitions
rows.imp_rat: Misassigning active tallies to the wrong case during transitions is a traceability bookkeeping error, peripheral to the physical grouping/loading job (low relevance); consequences are recoverable via reconciliation, but ceiling caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Manual tally tracking makes misassignment during transitions a recurring problem. Reassigning misattributed tallies costs reconciliation time. Keeping tallies with the right case during transitions requires operator attention. Wrong-case tallies cause count errors.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of closure material applied beyond the shipping-case sealing requirement
rows.imp_rat: Excess closure material is minor waste, peripheral to the loading job; trivial consequences kept low within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Sealers meter closure material fairly consistently, so overuse is minor and occasional. Excess closure application takes marginally more time. Applying the right amount depends on machine setting rather than operator skill. Overapplied tape or glue is direct consumable waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of batch-specific packaging discarded because its identity or release status cannot be confirmed
rows.imp_rat: Discarded batch-specific packaging is minor waste, peripheral to loading; trivial cost consequences kept low within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Status controls limit unnecessary discard, but unconfirmable materials still get scrapped recurrently. Resolving unconfirmable identity costs time before discard. Deciding to discard depends on operator judgment about status. Batch-specific packaging discarded for unconfirmable status is direct material waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of production-record entries whose case quantities disagree with physically packed quantities
rows.imp_rat: Record-vs-physical quantity disagreement is a reconciliation defect adjacent to loading (modest relevance); correctable and capped, so severity stays minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Manual record entry leaves recurring disagreements between recorded and physical quantities. Resolving record-vs-physical count disagreements costs time. Accurate record entry depends on operator care. Count discrepancies trigger costly investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of packed cases leaving tertiary packaging without a completed integrity decision
rows.imp_rat: Cases leaving without a completed integrity decision is a quality-gate omission somewhat related to loading completion (low relevance); capped by the batch ceiling regardless of downstream risk.
rows.sat_rat: Procedural sign-offs mostly ensure decisions, but cases occasionally leave without a completed check. Confirming an integrity decision before release adds brief time. Ensuring each case has a completed decision relies on operator discipline. Cases released without decision risk costly downstream failures.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of doubt that a closed shipping case contains the specified carton quantity
rows.imp_rat: Doubt that a closed case contains the specified count touches the loading correctness (slight relevance) but is a confidence/assurance concern that the ceiling caps low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of exposure to hazardous case-packer motion while diagnosing a stoppage or misload
rows.imp_rat: Exposure to hazardous motion during diagnosis has real severity, but it is peripheral to physical loading and the batch ceiling caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Diagnosing with guards defeated is common practice, leaving meaningful exposure risk. Exposure to hazardous motion during diagnosis can halt work for safety. Safe diagnosis needs trained energy-control judgement. The stake is injury, not material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator injury while accessing or adjusting the case packer during changeover
rows.imp_rat: Operator injury during changeover access has genuine severity but is peripheral to executing the case load and held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Guarding and procedures make changeover injuries relatively rare, though pinch-point exposure remains. Injury during changeover access causes significant lost time. Safe access relies on trained procedure adherence. Injury cost dominates over material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unexpected case-packer motion exposing the operator to injury during maintenance
rows.imp_rat: Unexpected motion exposing operator during maintenance carries safety stakes, yet is tangential to the loading job and capped by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: LOTO is a mature, enforced practice that makes unexpected-motion injury during maintenance uncommon. Injury from unexpected motion during maintenance causes major downtime. Safe maintenance depends on trained lockout/tagout practice. Injury cost far exceeds material considerations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator injury while correcting the identified case-packer fault
rows.imp_rat: Injury while correcting a fault carries safety severity but is marginal to the grouping/loading CFJ and capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Fault correction often tempts bypassing safeguards, keeping residual injury risk moderate. Injury during fault correction extends downtime substantially. Safe fault correction requires trained hazard awareness. Injury cost dominates.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a hazardous safeguard failure remains undetected during pre-production verification
rows.imp_rat: Undetected hazardous safeguard failure has real safety stakes (slightly higher severity) but is a pre-production verification reliability issue not central to the loading CFJ, and the ceiling caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Without rigorous functional safety testing, hazardous failures can remain undetected before production. Full pre-production safety verification takes time that is sometimes shortened. Detecting hazardous failures needs specialized safety testing skill. An undetected hazardous failure risks severe injury and liability, not just material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unexpected case-packer motion during reset after safeguard actuation
rows.imp_rat: Unexpected motion during safeguard reset poses safety severity, but it is peripheral to the grouping/loading CFJ and capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Reset-after-safeguard logic is generally engineered but unexpected motion incidents still occur. Unexpected motion during reset causes emergency stops that lose time. Safe reset requires trained understanding of stored energy and reset logic. The cost is injury risk, not material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a packaging configuration change is implemented without required regulatory assessment or approval
rows.imp_rat: Implementing a config change without regulatory assessment is a compliance-governance error, tangential to the physical CFJ of loading cartons (low relevance); severity capped despite potential regulatory exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control systems generally force regulatory review, making unassessed changes fairly rare in regulated firms. Regulatory assessment gates add lead time before change implementation. Determining when regulatory approval is required needs regulatory judgement. Unassessed changes risk compliance actions far costlier than the assessment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in acceptance criteria for grouping and loading cartoned units into shipping cases
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in acceptance criteria for grouping/loading touches the CFJ definition (modest relevance) but its consequences are indirect and capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Template-driven criteria help, but residual ambiguity in grouping/loading criteria persists in practice. Clarifying acceptance criteria is upfront work that does not chiefly cost time. Writing unambiguous acceptance criteria for grouping and loading requires experienced protocol authors. Ambiguity causes rework rather than direct material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of finished cases with counts or grouping patterns outside validated acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Finished cases with counts/patterns outside criteria touches loading correctness (slight relevance) but as an outcome defect it is capped low by the batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Automated count verification and rejection are mature, so out-of-criteria finished cases are relatively rare. Count/grouping checks run inline without meaningful added time. Modern case packers automate counting so little operator skill is required. Out-of-criteria cases cause reject and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartoned units damaged during routine grouping and loading into shipping cases
rows.imp_rat: Cartoned units damaged during routine grouping/loading directly relates to load quality (slight relevance) but as an outcome defect the batch ranking caps it low.
rows.sat_rat: Well-designed case packers keep routine damage low, so this is comparatively well served. Damage during routine loading does not primarily cost time. Minimizing handling damage depends on equipment tuning and operator skill. Damaged cartoned units are direct scrap and material loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of misidentifying the source of a Case Packer stoppage
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying a stoppage source is a diagnostic ambiguity, marginal to executing the load and capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Diagnostics are largely experience-based, so misidentifying causes is a recurring problem. Diagnosing stoppage source costs troubleshooting time. Correctly identifying the true stoppage cause demands significant operator or technician expertise. Misdiagnosis wastes effort and causes repeat stoppages.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of Case Packer stoppages recurring from an unresolved cause after restart
rows.imp_rat: Recurring stoppages from unresolved causes affect uptime but are peripheral to the grouping/loading job definition; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Without root-cause tools, stoppages recur from unresolved causes at a persistent rate. Recurring stoppages from unresolved causes repeatedly cost time. Preventing recurrence depends on operator's diagnostic skill. Repeat stoppages waste throughput.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of duplicate tally entries created for the same cartoned unit
rows.imp_rat: Duplicate tally entries for the same unit are a data-hygiene defect that doesn't affect actual grouping/loading (low relevance) and are easily deduplicated (low severity), and ranking caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Manual counting allows recurring duplicate entries with limited automated checks. Removing duplicate tally entries costs reconciliation time. Avoiding double-counting depends on operator diligence. Duplicate entries distort counts.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of completed case-packing events omitted from the applicable production record
rows.imp_rat: Completed events omitted from the record is a documentation-completeness defect, peripheral to physical loading and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Mandatory records capture most events, though occasional omissions persist. Recovering omitted events costs later time. Recording every event depends on operator discipline. Omitted events create compliance costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of entering case-packing activity in the production record for the wrong batch
rows.imp_rat: Entering activity against the wrong batch is a record-attribution defect, peripheral to grouping/loading and capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Batch record structure reduces wrong-batch entries, but manual entry leaves recurring risk. Confirming the correct batch before entry adds brief time. Entering the right batch depends on operator care with records. Wrong-batch entries cause costly record corrections.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a correction obscuring the original entry, rationale, or performer attribution
rows.imp_rat: A correction obscuring the original entry is a record-integrity error peripheral to physically grouping cartons; low consequence per ranking.
rows.sat_rat: GMP correction rules (single line, initial, date) are well established, so obscuring originals is relatively uncommon. Proper correction practice adds negligible time. Correct single-line correction is a well-trained basic GMP behavior. Obscured corrections risk costly audit findings.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between completion of case-packing activity and entry in the applicable production record
rows.imp_rat: Delay between activity and record entry is a documentation-timeliness issue, marginal to physically loading cases and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Entries are often batched later, so entry lag is a recurring practice gap. Delay between activity and record entry is the core issue and often lingers. Timely entry depends on operator discipline. Delayed entries risk data reconstruction cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of case-packing entries missing required batch, case, date, or performer details
rows.imp_rat: Entries missing batch/case/date details is a data-completeness defect, peripheral to executing the load and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Structured forms reduce missing fields, but incomplete entries still recur. Completing missing entry details later costs time. Capturing all required fields depends on operator thoroughness. Incomplete entries drive costly rework and audit findings.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of packing interruptions, rejects, or reconciliation discrepancies omitted from the applicable production record
rows.imp_rat: Omitting interruptions/rejects from the record is a record-completeness defect, peripheral to the loading CFJ and held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Interruptions and rejects are frequently omitted from records, a persistent documentation gap. Reconstructing omitted interruptions and rejects costs time later. Capturing all events depends on operator memory and diligence. Omitted records cause reconciliation and compliance costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting a torn, crushed, punctured, or moisture-damaged shipping case
rows.imp_rat: Accepting damaged shipping cases directly defeats the tertiary-packaging job and risks product loss, but the batch ceiling forces relevance and severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Visual inspection catches obvious damage, but subtle moisture or puncture damage occasionally passes. Inspecting incoming cases for damage adds brief time. Detecting torn or damaged cases relies on operator visual inspection. Accepting damaged cases wastes material and risks product loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed by an unaccounted-for batch-record gap during audit scrutiny
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed by a batch-record gap during audit is a personal reputational worry peripheral to grouping cartons; held minimal by ceiling.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of anxiety while awaiting clarification of an ambiguous batch-record entry
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety awaiting clarification of an ambiguous batch-record entry is an emotional/documentation concern remote from grouping cartons; minimal consequence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of lingering doubt after signing the batch record and line-clearance checks as complete
rows.imp_rat: Lingering doubt after signing records is a personal emotional concern remote from grouping cartons; trivial stakes under the ranking.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety spikes as ungrouped cartoned units accumulate at the Case Packer
rows.imp_rat: Operator anxiety as units accumulate is an experiential instability, marginal to the loading CFJ and held at the ceiling.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when the upstream carton cadence changes unexpectedly
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure when carton cadence changes is a personal emotional-regulation concern peripheral to actually loading cases; minor consequence under the ceiling.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty about continuing packing or requesting intervention while carton flow changes
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about continuing packing vs. intervention is a decision-latency ambiguity, marginal to the loading CFJ and capped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty about whether the correct cartoned units entered a closed shipping case
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about correct units entering a closed case is a verification-confidence ambiguity, peripheral to executing the load and capped low.

rows.stmt: Diminish the degree of anxiety while awaiting clarification of a questionable shipping-case count
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety while awaiting a questionable shipping-case count clarification is a purely emotional/administrative state peripheral to physically grouping and loading cartons; consequences are minor discomfort, not job failure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a Case Packer procedural deviation being formally attributed to the operator

rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost when the batch record is challenged is a reputational concern peripheral to grouping cartons; minimal stakes under the ceiling.

rows.imp_rat: Supervisors bypassing the operator in clearance decisions is an authority concern peripheral to grouping cartons; held minimal by the ceiling.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of exclusion from high-scrutiny Case Packer assignments because compliance discipline is doubted

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a case mix-up, count discrepancy, or integrity defect being visibly attributed to the operator
rows.imp_rat: A mix-up or defect visibly attributed to the operator is a reputational concern peripheral to the loading job's execution; held minimal by the ordering.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost with the line supervisor and Quality after a case discrepancy
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost after a case discrepancy is a reputational concern peripheral to grouping cartons; minimal consequence per the ordering.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of the operator's case-acceptance judgments being second-guessed because packing consistency is doubted
rows.imp_rat: Case-acceptance judgments second-guessed due to doubted consistency is an evaluation-reputation concern peripheral to the loading job; held minimal by ordering.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of being bypassed for high-trust packing assignments because of a defect-associated reputation
rows.imp_rat: Being bypassed for high-trust assignments due to reputation is a career/reputation concern peripheral to physical loading; held minimal by ordering.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of attributing an upstream or downstream disturbance to the case packer
rows.imp_rat: Attributing an upstream/downstream disturbance to the case packer is a diagnostic ambiguity, marginal to the grouping/loading job and held low.
rows.sat_rat: Without integrated line diagnostics, disturbances are frequently misattributed to the case packer. Misattributing an upstream/downstream disturbance wastes diagnostic time. rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that original case-packer fault evidence is altered before evaluation
rows.imp_rat: Fault evidence altered before evaluation is a records-integrity ambiguity, tangential to physical loading and capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of affected cartoned units with uncertain identity or disposition during diagnosis
rows.imp_rat: Units with uncertain identity during diagnosis is a traceability-confidence issue, peripheral to grouping/loading and capped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that weak case-packer lifecycle economics are attributed to the purchase executor's supplier judgment by senior leadership or finance

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost with senior leadership and finance when negotiated case-packer terms are viewed as economically weak
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost over weak terms is a reputational waste distant from packing execution; capped at 1 on both.
