rows.imp_rat: Exclusion from future sourcing after the acquisition is a career/governance error remote from execution; capped at 1 on both.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that authorized case-packing capacity falls short of forecast cartoned-unit throughput at the expansion horizon
rows.imp_rat: Authorized capacity falling short of forecast throughput is a capacity-planning concern distant from grouping cases; low consequence per ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Capacity sizing practices exist but horizon forecast error keeps shortfall risk materially present. Sizing to the expansion horizon requires forecast alignment that extends planning. Matching authorized capacity to horizon throughput needs capacity-engineering judgement. Undersizing forces costly rework or a second acquisition, while oversizing wastes capital.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of planned carton and shipping-case formats that the authorized case-packing capability cannot accommodate
rows.imp_rat: Formats the capability cannot accommodate directly limits the job, but ranking caps relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Format-coverage checks are partly standard but future formats are frequently missed. Confirming format coverage adds specification-review time. Matching capability to all carton and case formats requires packaging-engineering knowledge. Uncovered formats force costly retrofits or added equipment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of delay between the site capacity need date and deployment of the authorized case-packing acquisition
rows.imp_rat: Delay between capacity need date and deployment is a project-timing error indirectly tied to the CFJ; both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Deployment timing against need dates is unreliable given procurement and qualification variability. Delay between the capacity need date and actual deployment is a common, painful schedule miss. Compressing acquisition-to-deployment lead time needs project-management expertise. Late deployment against a need date can force costly interim workarounds.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required line-interface, material-flow, validation, and facility constraints omitted from the authorized scope
rows.imp_rat: Omitted interface/facility constraints from scope is a scoping defect indirectly tied to capability; both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Scope completeness for interfaces and constraints is weak, so omissions recur. Enumerating interface and facility constraints adds engineering scoping time. Capturing line-interface and material-flow constraints requires cross-disciplinary engineering skill. Omitted constraints cause costly rework during integration.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of case-packing capacity duplicated by overlapping site investments
rows.imp_rat: Duplicated capacity from overlapping site investments is a portfolio-planning waste, peripheral to the job; capped at 1 on relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Portfolio overlap reviews are inconsistent, so redundant capacity can be funded unnoticed. Cross-checking the portfolio for overlap adds coordination time. Spotting duplicated capacity across investments requires portfolio-level visibility not held by one executor. Duplicated capacity is wasted capital spent twice for one need.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a site capacity strategy change makes the authorized case-packing acquisition misaligned before deployment
rows.imp_rat: A strategy change misaligning the acquisition before deployment is a planning-stability concern distant from physical packing; low consequence per ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: There is little mechanism linking evolving capacity strategy back to in-flight acquisitions, leaving this largely unmanaged. Nothing in the status quo speeds detection of a strategy shift before deployment, so misalignment surfaces late. Anticipating strategy changes requires strategic foresight beyond the executor's typical vantage. A pre-deployment misalignment can strand committed capital on an unneeded asset.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty in the production-volume and product-mix assumptions used to determine required case-packing capacity
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty in volume/mix assumptions for capacity is a planning-analysis concern peripheral to physical packing; trivial consequence per ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Forecast inputs are inherently volatile and rarely locked, so assumption uncertainty is a chronic, poorly mitigated pain. Chasing volume and product-mix forecasts delays finalizing required capacity. Interpreting demand forecasts into capacity assumptions requires forecasting and product-mix analytical skill often outside the executor's remit. Wrong volume assumptions cause over- or under-sizing, a costly capital misallocation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of projected case-packing benefits dependent on unavailable upstream or downstream line capacity
rows.imp_rat: Benefits dependent on unavailable line capacity is a dependency defect indirectly tied to the CFJ; both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Dependency of benefits on upstream/downstream capacity is rarely systematically checked, leaving this poorly served. Assessing upstream/downstream dependency adds line-analysis time. Judging benefit dependence on adjacent line capacity requires whole-line engineering insight. Benefits stranded by missing adjacent capacity waste the projected return.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of case-packer lifecycle cost omitted from the financial model
rows.imp_rat: Omitted lifecycle cost from the financial model is a planning defect, tangential to physically loading cases; ceiling holds relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Lifecycle costing is inconsistently applied, so material cost elements are routinely left out of the model. Building a full lifecycle-cost picture adds upfront analysis time. Total-cost-of-ownership modeling requires costing expertise often underdeveloped for capital requests. Omitted lifecycle costs directly understate true spend and erode realized returns.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of projected labor, throughput, and loss-reduction savings that is unsupported or double-counted
rows.imp_rat: Unsupported or double-counted savings is a financial-modeling defect distant from packing execution; capped at 1 on both.
rows.sat_rat: Savings verification is inconsistent, so double-counting and unsupported claims frequently survive review. Validating savings claims adds review scrutiny time. Detecting unsupported or double-counted savings requires financial-audit rigor not always available. Overstated savings inflate the case and risk funding based on phantom returns.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterile fill-finish quality and validation requirements omitted from the case-packer investment case
rows.imp_rat: Omitted sterile validation requirements from investment case is a regulatory-scope defect; capped at 1 on both by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Validation-requirement capture in the investment case is inconsistent, so omissions persist. Adding sterile quality/validation requirements to the case takes time. Specifying sterile validation requirements needs specialized quality expertise. Omitted validation requirements trigger unbudgeted qualification cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of delay between planned and achievable benefit realization for the case-packer investment
rows.imp_rat: Delay between planned and achievable benefit is a value-timing error peripheral to execution; ceiling forces both to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Achievable-versus-planned realization is seldom rigorously reconciled, so slippage persists. Delay between planned and achievable realization is a recurring, poorly controlled schedule gap. Closing this gap needs realistic ramp planning skill often lacking. Each month of delay defers return and extends payback.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty in the capital estimate submitted for case-packer authorization
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty in the capital estimate is an estimation-accuracy issue upstream of physical packing; low stakes under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Quote-based estimating is standard but early-stage case-packer scopes still carry material estimate uncertainty. Firming up a capital estimate requires vendor quotes and scoping that lengthen the authorization timeline. Producing a low-uncertainty estimate demands estimating expertise and vendor engagement not always resident in the role. An uncertain estimate directly threatens budget overrun or padded contingency that ties up capital.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of errors in cash-flow timing, depreciation, tax, and discounting calculations
rows.imp_rat: Errors in cash-flow/depreciation/tax calculations are financial-modeling defects distant from packing; capped at 1 on both.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized financial models make these calculations fairly reliable, with residual input errors. Standard financial templates make cash-flow and discounting calculations quick. Cash-flow, depreciation, tax and discounting errors are the calculation-competence risk this need targets, mitigated by established finance templates. Calculation errors can misstate return but tools largely prevent them.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency in the financial assumptions used to compare feasible case-packing alternatives
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent financial assumptions comparing alternatives is an evaluation-analysis concern peripheral to case loading; trivial consequence under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: There is no strong standard practice forcing common assumptions, so comparisons across case-packing alternatives remain frequently inconsistent. Reconciling divergent financial assumptions across alternatives adds review iterations before a decision can be made. Enforcing consistent cost-of-capital, inflation and horizon assumptions requires financial-analysis expertise unevenly available at site level. Inconsistent assumptions distort comparative economics and can steer capital toward a poorer option, wasting investment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of financial exposure from implementation, ramp-up, and demand risks omitted from the investment case
rows.imp_rat: Omitted implementation/ramp/demand risk from the investment case is a planning defect tangential to execution; relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Investment cases routinely omit structured risk quantification, so this exposure is poorly controlled. Risk quantification adds modeling time to the case build. Quantifying implementation, ramp-up and demand risk requires risk-analysis expertise in short supply. Omitted risk exposure understates potential financial loss on the investment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of unbudgeted expenditure for site modifications and sterile fill-finish line integration
rows.imp_rat: Unbudgeted site-integration spend is a project-cost waste peripheral to the packing job; both proxies at 1.

rows.imp_rat: Unplanned stoppages during ownership directly interrupt the job, but the batch ceiling forces relevance and severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Preventive maintenance practices exist but unplanned stoppages remain a recurring operational reality. Unplanned stoppages during ownership cause lost run time, addressed reactively. Reducing stoppages needs reliability engineering and maintenance capability that varies by site. Each stoppage carries lost-output and response cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of financial assumptions in the case-packer rationale lacking traceable evidence
rows.imp_rat: Assumptions lacking traceable evidence is a business-case ambiguity remote from execution; ceiling holds both at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Evidence traceability for assumptions is weak, so many assumptions lack documented backing. Tracing evidence for each assumption adds documentation time. rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of misalignment between projected case-packing capacity and the site capacity strategy

rows.sat_rat: Capacity planning inputs exist but reconciliation to strategy is inconsistent, leaving recurring misalignment risk. rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of investment value left unquantified across capacity, labor, and production-continuity benefits
rows.imp_rat: Unquantified investment value is a business-case ambiguity distant from the job; relevance and severity held at 1.

Valuing capacity, labor and continuity benefits requires benefits-quantification skill unevenly present. Unquantified value understates the true return and risks under-justifying worthwhile spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material product-quality, traceability, and validation risks absent from the investment rationale
rows.imp_rat: Missing product-quality/traceability risks in rationale is a business-case gap; ranking forces relevance and severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Quality-risk capture in investment rationale is inconsistent, leaving material risks unstated. Surfacing quality and traceability risks adds review effort. Identifying product-quality and validation risks requires quality-assurance expertise not resident in procurement. Absent quality risks can lead to expensive post-hoc fixes.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of decision-relevant investment alternatives evaluated on inconsistent financial bases
rows.imp_rat: Alternatives evaluated on inconsistent bases is an analysis-ambiguity error distant from packing; capped at 1 on both.
rows.sat_rat: Consistent evaluation frameworks are inconsistently enforced across alternatives. Standardizing evaluation bases adds harmonization time. rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicting financial and capacity figures presented to executive stakeholders
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting figures to executives is a reporting-ambiguity error distant from the packing job; both proxies at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variation in projected returns across credible demand, throughput, and operating-cost conditions
rows.imp_rat: Variation in projected returns across conditions is an investment-analysis concern distant from loading cartons; low stakes per the ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Scenario analysis is inconsistently applied, so projected returns are often presented as point estimates with hidden variability. Running multi-scenario return analyses adds analytical cycles to the review. Sensitivity and scenario modeling across demand and cost conditions demands financial-modeling expertise seldom held by the executor. Unexamined return variation risks funding a project that underperforms, exposing invested capital.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of final approval delay caused by unresolved executive objections
rows.imp_rat: Approval delay from executive objections is an authorization-timing error tangential to the job; both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-alignment practices help but late executive objections still unpredictably stall sign-off. Unresolved executive objections directly stall final approval, a recurring delay. Preempting objections requires stakeholder-management and persuasion skill. Approval delay defers spend authority but has limited direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of financial and capacity impact from deferring the case-packer investment left unquantified
rows.imp_rat: Unquantified deferral impact is a planning-ambiguity error remote from the packing job; ceiling caps relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Deferral-cost analysis is largely absent from standard practice, leaving this need chronically unserved. Quantifying deferral impact takes analysis rarely prioritized under time pressure. Modeling the cost of delay requires economic-analysis skill seldom applied to deferral scenarios. rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of return forgone by funding case packing instead of the best competing capital option
rows.imp_rat: Forgone return versus competing capital is a portfolio-allocation waste far from execution; capped at 1 on both.
rows.sat_rat: Opportunity-cost comparison is inconsistently performed, leaving this only partly managed. Comparing against the best alternative adds analytical time. rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of funded case-packing capacity left unused over the investment horizon

rows.sat_rat: Utilization forecasting is inconsistent, so stranded-capacity risk is only loosely managed. Projecting utilization over the horizon adds analytical time. Forecasting utilization needs demand and capacity modeling skill. Unused funded capacity is idle capital — a direct waste.

rows.imp_rat: Another line constraint preventing added capacity from increasing output is a throughput-planning concern distant from grouping cases; minimal stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Whole-line constraint analysis is inconsistently done, so this failure mode recurs unnoticed until after install. Line-balancing studies to expose other constraints take time that reviews rarely allow. Identifying whether an upstream/downstream bottleneck negates added capacity needs line-engineering expertise. rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between capital deployment and realization of expected case-packing benefits
rows.imp_rat: Duration to benefit realization is a value-timing delay tangential to the CFJ; relevance and severity capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Benefit-realization timing is inconsistently managed, so lags recur. The gap between deployment and benefit realization is the core delay this need targets and is often longer than planned. Accelerating benefit capture requires ramp-up and commissioning expertise not always coordinated. Delayed benefits defer return on deployed capital, effectively a carrying cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of planned product and shipping-case formats that the authorized case-packing capability cannot accommodate
rows.imp_rat: Product/case formats the capability cannot handle limits the job, yet the batch ceiling forces both to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Product/case format coverage is inconsistently validated against the future portfolio. Verifying product and case-format coverage adds specification time. Confirming format compatibility needs packaging and product-portfolio expertise. Unaccommodated formats drive expensive change or new capital.

Anticipating redesign/requal needs demands validation and engineering foresight not always applied at authorization. Redesign and requalification generate substantial unplanned expenditure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the case-packer financial case fails the required investment-return hurdle
rows.imp_rat: The financial case failing the return hurdle is an investment-analysis concern distant from physical loading; low consequence per ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Hurdle evaluation is well-established, though its validity depends on assumption quality that varies. Hurdle-rate testing is a standard gated step that rarely adds delay once inputs are ready. Applying the investment-return hurdle is routine finance practice with clear templates. A failed hurdle signals capital that would be poorly returned, and the test guards against that.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of delay beyond the authorization date required by the site capacity strategy

Locking assumptions early requires forecasting discipline often not enforced. Late assumption changes can invalidate cost sizing already committed.
